question_html: Find the values of p and q for which {tex}f ( x ) = \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \frac { 1 - \sin ^ { 3 } x } { 3 \cos ^ { 2 } x } , } & { \text { if } x < \frac { \pi } { 2 } } \\ { p , } & { \text { if } x = \frac { \pi } { 2 } } \\ { \frac { q ( 1 - \sin x ) } { ( \pi - 2 x ) ^ { 2 } } , } & { \text { if } x > \frac { \pi } { 2 } } \end{array} \right.{/tex}is continuous at x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}.
question_text: Find the values of p and q for which {tex}f ( x ) = \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \frac { 1 - \sin ^ { 3 } x } { 3 \cos ^ { 2 } x } , } & { \text { if } x < \frac { \pi } { 2 } } \\ { p , } & { \text { if } x = \frac { \pi } { 2 } } \\ { \frac { q ( 1 - \sin x ) } { ( \pi - 2 x ) ^ { 2 } } , } & { \text { if } x > \frac { \pi } { 2 } } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} .
explanation: Given, f(x) = {tex}\left\{ \begin{aligned} \frac { 1 - \sin ^ { 3 } x } { 3 \cos ^ { 2 } x } , & \text { if } x < \frac { \pi } { 2 } \\ p , & \text { if } x = \frac { \pi } { 2 } \\ \frac { q ( 1 - \sin x ) } { ( \pi - 2 x ) ^ { 2 } } , & \text { if } x > \frac { \pi } { 2 } \end{aligned} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = {tex}\frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} We shall use definition of continuity to find the values of constants a and b. Since f(x) is continuous at x= {tex}\frac{ {tex}\pi{/tex} }{2}{/tex} , Therefore, {tex}(LHL)_ { x = \frac { \pi } { 2 } } = ( R H L ) _ { x = \frac { \pi } { 2 } } = f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} ........(i) Now, LHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow \frac { \pi } { 2 } ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - h \right){/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 1 - \sin ^ { 3 } \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - h \right) } { 3 \cos ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - h \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 1 - \cos ^ { 3 } h } { 3 \sin ^ { 2 } h }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { ( 1 - \cos h ) \left( 1 ^ { 2 } + \cos ^ { 2 } h + 1 \times \cos h \right) } { 3 \left( 1 - \cos ^ { 2 } h \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { ( 1 - \cos h ) \left( 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } h + \cos h \right) } { 3 ( 1 - \cos h ) ( 1 + \cos h ) }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \left( 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } h + \cos h \right) } { 3 ( 1 + \cos h ) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } 0 + \cos 0 } { 3 ( 1 + \cos 0 ) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 + 1 + 1 } { 3 ( 1 + 1 ) } = \frac { 3 } { 3 \times 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} .......(ii) and RHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow \frac { \pi } { 2 } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } + h \right){/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { q \left[ 1 - \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } + h \right) \right] } { \left[ \pi - 2 \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } + h \right) \right] ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { q ( 1 - \cos h ) } { ( \pi - \pi - 2 h ) ^ { 2 } } = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { q ( 1 - \cos h ) } { 4 h ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { q \left( 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { h } { 2 } \right) } { 4 h ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \cos x = 1 - 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { x } { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { q } { 8 } \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \left[ \frac { \sin \left( \frac { h } { 2 } \right) } { \frac { h } { 2 } } \right] ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { q } { 8 } \times 1 = \frac { q } { 8 }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin x } { x } = 1 \right]{/tex} ...........(iii) On substituting the values from Eqs. (ii) and (iii) to Eq.(i), we get {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } = \frac { q } { 8 } = f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 2 } = \frac { q } { 8 } = p{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) = p ( \text { given } ) \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 2 } = \frac { q } { 8 }{/tex} and {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } = p{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} q = 4 and p = {tex}\frac 12{/tex}
solution: Given, f(x) = {tex}\left\{ \begin{aligned} \frac { 1 - \sin ^ { 3 } x } { 3 \cos ^ { 2 } x } , & \text { if } x < \frac { \pi } { 2 } \\ p , & \text { if } x = \frac { \pi } { 2 } \\ \frac { q ( 1 - \sin x ) } { ( \pi - 2 x ) ^ { 2 } } , & \text { if } x > \frac { \pi } { 2 } \end{aligned} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = {tex}\frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} We shall use definition of continuity to find the values of constants a and b. Since f(x) is continuous at x= {tex}\frac{ {tex}\pi{/tex} }{2}{/tex} , Therefore, {tex}(LHL)_ { x = \frac { \pi } { 2 } } = ( R H L ) _ { x = \frac { \pi } { 2 } } = f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} ........(i) Now, LHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow \frac { \pi } { 2 } ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - h \right){/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 1 - \sin ^ { 3 } \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - h \right) } { 3 \cos ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - h \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 1 - \cos ^ { 3 } h } { 3 \sin ^ { 2 } h }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { ( 1 - \cos h ) \left( 1 ^ { 2 } + \cos ^ { 2 } h + 1 \times \cos h \right) } { 3 \left( 1 - \cos ^ { 2 } h \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { ( 1 - \cos h ) \left( 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } h + \cos h \right) } { 3 ( 1 - \cos h ) ( 1 + \cos h ) }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \left( 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } h + \cos h \right) } { 3 ( 1 + \cos h ) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } 0 + \cos 0 } { 3 ( 1 + \cos 0 ) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 + 1 + 1 } { 3 ( 1 + 1 ) } = \frac { 3 } { 3 \times 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} .......(ii) and RHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow \frac { \pi } { 2 } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } + h \right){/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { q \left[ 1 - \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } + h \right) \right] } { \left[ \pi - 2 \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } + h \right) \right] ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { q ( 1 - \cos h ) } { ( \pi - \pi - 2 h ) ^ { 2 } } = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { q ( 1 - \cos h ) } { 4 h ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { q \left( 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { h } { 2 } \right) } { 4 h ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \cos x = 1 - 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { x } { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { q } { 8 } \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \left[ \frac { \sin \left( \frac { h } { 2 } \right) } { \frac { h } { 2 } } \right] ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { q } { 8 } \times 1 = \frac { q } { 8 }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin x } { x } = 1 \right]{/tex} ...........(iii) On substituting the values from Eqs. (ii) and (iii) to Eq.(i), we get {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } = \frac { q } { 8 } = f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 2 } = \frac { q } { 8 } = p{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) = p ( \text { given } ) \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 2 } = \frac { q } { 8 }{/tex} and {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } = p{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} q = 4 and p = {tex}\frac 12{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, f(x) = {tex}\left\{ \begin{aligned} \frac { 1 - \sin ^ { 3 } x } { 3 \cos ^ { 2 } x } , & \text { if } x < \frac { \pi } { 2 } \\ p , & \text { if } x = \frac { \pi } { 2 } \\ \frac { q ( 1 - \sin x ) } { ( \pi - 2 x ) ^ { 2 } } , & \text { if } x > \frac { \pi } { 2 } \end{aligned} \right.{/tex}is continuous at x = {tex}\frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex}

 We shall use definition of continuity to find the values of constants a and b.

Since f(x) is continuous at x={tex}\frac{{tex}\pi{/tex}}{2}{/tex},
Therefore,{tex}(LHL)_ { x = \frac { \pi } { 2 } } = ( R H L ) _ { x = \frac { \pi } { 2 } } = f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex}........(i)
Now, LHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow \frac { \pi } { 2 } ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - h \right){/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 1 - \sin ^ { 3 } \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - h \right) } { 3 \cos ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - h \right) }{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 1 - \cos ^ { 3 } h } { 3 \sin ^ { 2 } h }{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { ( 1 - \cos h ) \left( 1 ^ { 2 } + \cos ^ { 2 } h + 1 \times \cos h \right) } { 3 \left( 1 - \cos ^ { 2 } h \right) }{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { ( 1 - \cos h ) \left( 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } h + \cos h \right) } { 3 ( 1 - \cos h ) ( 1 + \cos h ) }{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \left( 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } h + \cos h \right) } { 3 ( 1 + \cos h ) }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } 0 + \cos 0 } { 3 ( 1 + \cos 0 ) }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 + 1 + 1 } { 3 ( 1 + 1 ) } = \frac { 3 } { 3 \times 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}.......(ii)
and RHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow \frac { \pi } { 2 } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } + h \right){/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { q \left[ 1 - \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } + h \right) \right] } { \left[ \pi - 2 \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } + h \right) \right] ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { q ( 1 - \cos h ) } { ( \pi - \pi - 2 h ) ^ { 2 } } = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { q ( 1 - \cos h ) } { 4 h ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { q \left( 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { h } { 2 } \right) } { 4 h ^ { 2 } }{/tex}{tex}\left[ \because \cos x = 1 - 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { x } { 2 } \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { q } { 8 } \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \left[ \frac { \sin \left( \frac { h } { 2 } \right) } { \frac { h } { 2 } } \right] ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { q } { 8 } \times 1 = \frac { q } { 8 }{/tex}{tex}\left[ \because \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin x } { x } = 1 \right]{/tex}...........(iii)
On substituting the values from Eqs. (ii) and (iii) to Eq.(i), we get
{tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } = \frac { q } { 8 } = f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 2 } = \frac { q } { 8 } = p{/tex}{tex}\left[ \because f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) = p ( \text { given } ) \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 2 } = \frac { q } { 8 }{/tex} and {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } = p{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex}q = 4 and p = {tex}\frac 12{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}{(x - a)^2} + {(y - b)^2} = {c^2}{/tex}Prove {tex}\frac{{{{\left[ {1 + {{\left( {\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right)}^2}} \right]}^{3/2}}}}{{\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}}}}{/tex}is a constant independent of a & b.
question_text: If {tex}{(x - a)^2} + {(y - b)^2} = {c^2}{/tex} Prove {tex}\frac{{{{\left[ {1 + {{\left( {\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right)}^2}} \right]}^{3/2}}}}{{\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}}}}{/tex} is a constant independent of a & b.
explanation: {tex}{(x - a)^2} + {(y - b)^2} = {c^2}{/tex} (Given) ....(1) Differentiating both sides w.r.t. to x {tex}2(x - a) + 2(y - b){y_1} = 0{/tex} {tex}(x - a) + (y - b){y_1} = 0{/tex} ...(2) Again differentiating both sides, {tex}(1 - 0) + (y - b){y_2} + {y_1}.{y_1} = 0{/tex} {tex}1 + (y - b){y_2} + y_1^2 = 0{/tex} {tex}y - b = \frac{{ - 1 - y_1^2}}{{{y_2}}}{/tex} Put (y-b) in equation (1) {tex}(x - a) +\left( {\frac{{1 + y_1^2}}{{{y_2}}}} \right){y_1} = 0{/tex} {tex}x - a = -\left( {\frac{{1 + y_1^2}}{{{y_2}}}} \right).{y_1}{/tex} Put the value of (x - a) and (y - b) in equation (1) {tex}{\left[ {\left( {\frac{{1 + y_1^2}}{{{y_2}}}} \right){y_1}} \right]^2} + {\left( {\frac{{1 + y_1^2}}{{{y_2}}}} \right)^2} = {c^2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{\left( {(1 + y_1^2} )^2 \right)y_1^2}}{{y_2^2}} + \frac{{{{\left( {1 + y_1^2} \right)}^2}}}{{y_2^2}} = {c^2}{/tex} {tex} \pm \sqrt {\frac{{{{\left( {1 + y_1^2} \right)}^3}}}{{y_{^2}^2}}}{/tex} = c {tex}\frac{{{{\left[ {{{\left( {1 + \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right)}^2}} \right]}^{3/2}}}}{{\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}}}} = c{/tex} Hence proved.
solution: {tex}{(x - a)^2} + {(y - b)^2} = {c^2}{/tex} (Given) ....(1) Differentiating both sides w.r.t. to x {tex}2(x - a) + 2(y - b){y_1} = 0{/tex} {tex}(x - a) + (y - b){y_1} = 0{/tex} ...(2) Again differentiating both sides, {tex}(1 - 0) + (y - b){y_2} + {y_1}.{y_1} = 0{/tex} {tex}1 + (y - b){y_2} + y_1^2 = 0{/tex} {tex}y - b = \frac{{ - 1 - y_1^2}}{{{y_2}}}{/tex} Put (y-b) in equation (1) {tex}(x - a) +\left( {\frac{{1 + y_1^2}}{{{y_2}}}} \right){y_1} = 0{/tex} {tex}x - a = -\left( {\frac{{1 + y_1^2}}{{{y_2}}}} \right).{y_1}{/tex} Put the value of (x - a) and (y - b) in equation (1) {tex}{\left[ {\left( {\frac{{1 + y_1^2}}{{{y_2}}}} \right){y_1}} \right]^2} + {\left( {\frac{{1 + y_1^2}}{{{y_2}}}} \right)^2} = {c^2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{\left( {(1 + y_1^2} )^2 \right)y_1^2}}{{y_2^2}} + \frac{{{{\left( {1 + y_1^2} \right)}^2}}}{{y_2^2}} = {c^2}{/tex} {tex} \pm \sqrt {\frac{{{{\left( {1 + y_1^2} \right)}^3}}}{{y_{^2}^2}}}{/tex} = c {tex}\frac{{{{\left[ {{{\left( {1 + \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right)}^2}} \right]}^{3/2}}}}{{\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}}}} = c{/tex} Hence proved.
solution_html:
{tex}{(x - a)^2} + {(y - b)^2} = {c^2}{/tex} (Given) ....(1)

Differentiating both sides w.r.t. to x

{tex}2(x - a) + 2(y - b){y_1} = 0{/tex}

{tex}(x - a) + (y - b){y_1} = 0{/tex}...(2)

Again differentiating both sides,

{tex}(1 - 0) + (y - b){y_2} + {y_1}.{y_1} = 0{/tex}

{tex}1 + (y - b){y_2} + y_1^2 = 0{/tex}

{tex}y - b = \frac{{ - 1 - y_1^2}}{{{y_2}}}{/tex}

Put (y-b) in equation (1)

{tex}(x - a) +\left( {\frac{{1 + y_1^2}}{{{y_2}}}} \right){y_1} = 0{/tex}

{tex}x - a = -\left( {\frac{{1 + y_1^2}}{{{y_2}}}} \right).{y_1}{/tex}

Put the value of (x - a) and (y - b) in equation (1)

{tex}{\left[ {\left( {\frac{{1 + y_1^2}}{{{y_2}}}} \right){y_1}} \right]^2} + {\left( {\frac{{1 + y_1^2}}{{{y_2}}}} \right)^2} = {c^2}{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{\left( {(1 + y_1^2} )^2 \right)y_1^2}}{{y_2^2}} + \frac{{{{\left( {1 + y_1^2} \right)}^2}}}{{y_2^2}} = {c^2}{/tex}

{tex} \pm \sqrt {\frac{{{{\left( {1 + y_1^2} \right)}^3}}}{{y_{^2}^2}}}{/tex}= c

 

{tex}\frac{{{{\left[ {{{\left( {1 + \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right)}^2}} \right]}^{3/2}}}}{{\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}}}} = c{/tex}

Hence proved.

question_html: If x = a (cos{tex}\theta{/tex} + {tex}\theta{/tex} sin{tex}\theta{/tex}), y = a (sin{tex}\theta{/tex} - {tex}\theta{/tex}cos{tex}\theta{/tex}), prove that {tex}\frac{d^{2} x}{d \theta^{2}}=a(\cos \theta-\theta \sin \theta), \frac{d^{2} y}{d \theta^{2}}=a(\sin \theta+\theta \cos \theta){/tex} and {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\sec ^{3} \theta}{a \theta}{/tex}
question_text: If x = a (cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + {tex}\theta{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ), y = a (sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - {tex}\theta{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ), prove that {tex}\frac{d^{2} x}{d \theta^{2}}=a(\cos \theta-\theta \sin \theta), \frac{d^{2} y}{d \theta^{2}}=a(\sin \theta+\theta \cos \theta){/tex} and {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\sec ^{3} \theta}{a \theta}{/tex}
explanation: Given, x = a (cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + {tex}\theta{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ) ...(1) y = a (sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - {tex}\theta{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ) ...(2) To prove: (i) {tex}\frac{d^{2} x}{d \theta^{2}}=a(\cos \theta-\theta \sin \theta),{/tex} (ii) {tex} \frac{d^{2} y}{d \theta^{2}}=a(\sin \theta+\theta \cos \theta){/tex} (iii) {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\sec ^{3} \theta}{a \theta}{/tex} We notice a second order derivative in the expression to be proved so first take the step to find the second order derivative. Let's find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} As {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}=\frac{d}{d \theta} a(\cos \theta+\theta \sin \theta){/tex} = a (-sin {tex}\theta{/tex} + {tex}\theta{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ) [differentiated using product rule for {tex}\theta{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ] = a {tex}\theta{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ...(3) Again differentiating w.r.t. {tex}\theta{/tex} using product rule: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{d} \theta^{2}}=\mathrm{a}(-\theta \sin \theta+\cos \theta){/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{d} \theta^{2}}=\mathrm{a}(\cos \theta-\theta \sin \theta){/tex} ...proved (i) Similarly, {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{d} \theta}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} \theta} \mathrm{a}(\sin \theta-\theta \cos \theta)=\mathrm{a} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} \theta} \sin \theta-\mathrm{a} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} \theta}(\theta \cos \theta){/tex} = a cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + a {tex}\theta{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - a cos {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d \theta}=a \theta \sin \theta{/tex} ...(5) Again differentiating w.r.t {tex}\theta{/tex} using product rule: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{d} \theta^{2}}=\mathrm{a}(\theta \cos \theta+\sin \theta){/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{d} \theta^{2}}=\mathrm{a}(\sin \theta+\theta \cos \theta){/tex} ...proved (ii) {tex}\because \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d \theta}}{\frac{d x}{d \theta}}{/tex} Using equations (4) and (5) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{a \theta \sin \theta}{a \theta \cos \theta}=\tan \theta{/tex} As {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Again differentiating w.r.t x: {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x} \tan \theta{/tex} {tex}=\sec ^{2} \theta \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{d} x}{/tex} [using chain rule] {tex}\because \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{d} \theta}=\mathrm{a} \theta \cos \theta=>\frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{a} \theta \cos \theta}{/tex} Putting a value in the above equation We have: {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\sec ^{2} \theta \times \frac{1}{a \theta \cos \theta}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\sec ^{3} \theta}{a \theta}{/tex} ...proved (iii)
solution: Given, x = a (cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + {tex}\theta{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ) ...(1) y = a (sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - {tex}\theta{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ) ...(2) To prove: (i) {tex}\frac{d^{2} x}{d \theta^{2}}=a(\cos \theta-\theta \sin \theta),{/tex} (ii) {tex} \frac{d^{2} y}{d \theta^{2}}=a(\sin \theta+\theta \cos \theta){/tex} (iii) {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\sec ^{3} \theta}{a \theta}{/tex} We notice a second order derivative in the expression to be proved so first take the step to find the second order derivative. Let's find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} As {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}=\frac{d}{d \theta} a(\cos \theta+\theta \sin \theta){/tex} = a (-sin {tex}\theta{/tex} + {tex}\theta{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ) [differentiated using product rule for {tex}\theta{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ] = a {tex}\theta{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ...(3) Again differentiating w.r.t. {tex}\theta{/tex} using product rule: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{d} \theta^{2}}=\mathrm{a}(-\theta \sin \theta+\cos \theta){/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{d} \theta^{2}}=\mathrm{a}(\cos \theta-\theta \sin \theta){/tex} ...proved (i) Similarly, {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{d} \theta}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} \theta} \mathrm{a}(\sin \theta-\theta \cos \theta)=\mathrm{a} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} \theta} \sin \theta-\mathrm{a} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} \theta}(\theta \cos \theta){/tex} = a cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + a {tex}\theta{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - a cos {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d \theta}=a \theta \sin \theta{/tex} ...(5) Again differentiating w.r.t {tex}\theta{/tex} using product rule: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{d} \theta^{2}}=\mathrm{a}(\theta \cos \theta+\sin \theta){/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{d} \theta^{2}}=\mathrm{a}(\sin \theta+\theta \cos \theta){/tex} ...proved (ii) {tex}\because \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d \theta}}{\frac{d x}{d \theta}}{/tex} Using equations (4) and (5) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{a \theta \sin \theta}{a \theta \cos \theta}=\tan \theta{/tex} As {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Again differentiating w.r.t x: {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x} \tan \theta{/tex} {tex}=\sec ^{2} \theta \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{d} x}{/tex} [using chain rule] {tex}\because \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{d} \theta}=\mathrm{a} \theta \cos \theta=>\frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{a} \theta \cos \theta}{/tex} Putting a value in the above equation We have: {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\sec ^{2} \theta \times \frac{1}{a \theta \cos \theta}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\sec ^{3} \theta}{a \theta}{/tex} ...proved (iii)
solution_html:
Given,
x = a (cos{tex}\theta{/tex} + {tex}\theta{/tex} sin{tex}\theta{/tex}) ...(1)
y = a (sin{tex}\theta{/tex} - {tex}\theta{/tex} cos{tex}\theta{/tex}) ...(2)
To prove:
(i) {tex}\frac{d^{2} x}{d \theta^{2}}=a(\cos \theta-\theta \sin \theta),{/tex}
(ii)  {tex} \frac{d^{2} y}{d \theta^{2}}=a(\sin \theta+\theta \cos \theta){/tex}
(iii) {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\sec ^{3} \theta}{a \theta}{/tex}
We notice a second order derivative in the expression to be proved so first take the step to find the second order derivative.
Let's find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex}
As {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}=\frac{d}{d \theta} a(\cos \theta+\theta \sin \theta){/tex}
= a (-sin{tex}\theta{/tex} + {tex}\theta{/tex} cos{tex}\theta{/tex} + sin{tex}\theta{/tex})
[differentiated using product rule for {tex}\theta{/tex} sin{tex}\theta{/tex}]
= a{tex}\theta{/tex} cos{tex}\theta{/tex} ...(3)
Again differentiating w.r.t. {tex}\theta{/tex} using product rule:
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{d} \theta^{2}}=\mathrm{a}(-\theta \sin \theta+\cos \theta){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{d} \theta^{2}}=\mathrm{a}(\cos \theta-\theta \sin \theta){/tex} ...proved (i)
Similarly,
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{d} \theta}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} \theta} \mathrm{a}(\sin \theta-\theta \cos \theta)=\mathrm{a} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} \theta} \sin \theta-\mathrm{a} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} \theta}(\theta \cos \theta){/tex}
= a cos{tex}\theta{/tex} + a {tex}\theta{/tex} sin{tex}\theta{/tex} - a cos{tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d \theta}=a \theta \sin \theta{/tex} ...(5)
Again differentiating w.r.t {tex}\theta{/tex} using product rule:
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{d} \theta^{2}}=\mathrm{a}(\theta \cos \theta+\sin \theta){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{d} \theta^{2}}=\mathrm{a}(\sin \theta+\theta \cos \theta){/tex} ...proved (ii)
{tex}\because \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d \theta}}{\frac{d x}{d \theta}}{/tex}
Using equations (4) and (5)
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{a \theta \sin \theta}{a \theta \cos \theta}=\tan \theta{/tex}
As {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Again differentiating w.r.t x:
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x} \tan \theta{/tex}
{tex}=\sec ^{2} \theta \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{d} x}{/tex} [using chain rule]
{tex}\because \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{d} \theta}=\mathrm{a} \theta \cos \theta=>\frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{a} \theta \cos \theta}{/tex}
Putting a value in the above equation
We have:
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\sec ^{2} \theta \times \frac{1}{a \theta \cos \theta}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\sec ^{3} \theta}{a \theta}{/tex} ...proved (iii)

\sin x} \right)} \right] + \left( {\log x + \log \cos x} \right).1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{u}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} {/tex} {tex} = \left[ {1 - x\tan x + \log \left( {x\cos x} \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{du}}{{dx}} = u\left[ {1 - x\tan x + \log \left( {x\cos x} \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{du}}{{dx}} = {\left( {x\cos x} \right)^x}\left[ {1 - x\tan x + \log \left( {x\cos x} \right)} \right]{/tex} .....(ii) Again {tex}v = {\left( {x\sin x} \right)^{\frac{1}{x}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log v = \log {\left( {x\sin x} \right)^{\frac{1}{x}}} = \frac{1}{x}\log \left( {x\sin x} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log v = \frac{1}{x}\left( {\log x + \log \sin x} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d}{{dx}}\log v = \frac{d}{{dx}}\left\{ {\frac{1}{x}\left( {\log x + \log \sin x} \right)} \right\}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}\frac{dv}{{dx}} {/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{x}\left[ {\frac{1}{x} + \frac{1}{{\ sinx }}.\cos x} \right] + \left( {\log x + \log \sin x} \right)\left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{{{x^2}}}} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}\frac{dv}{{dx}} {/tex} {tex}= \left[ {\frac{1}{{{x^2}}} + \frac{{\cot x}}{x} - \frac{{\log \left( {x\sin x} \right)}}{{{x^2}}}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{dv}{{dx}} = v\left[ {\frac{1}{{{x^2}}} + \frac{{\cot x}}{x} - \frac{{\log \left( {x\sin x} \right)}}{{{x^2}}}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{dv}{{dx}} = {\left( {x\sin x} \right)^{\frac{1}{x}}}\left[ {\frac{1}{{{x^2}}} + \frac{{\cot x}}{x} - \frac{{\log \left( {x\sin x} \right)}}{{{x^2}}}} \right]{/tex} ...(iii) Putting the values from eq. (ii) and (iii) in eq. (i) {tex}\frac{d}{{dx}} = {\left( {x\cos x} \right)^{^x}}{/tex} {tex}\left[ {1 - x\tan x + \log \left( {x\cos x} \right)} \right] + {\left( {x\sin x} \right)^{\frac{1}{x}}}{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\frac{1}{{{x^2}}} + \frac{{\cot x}}{x} - \frac{{\log \left( {x\sin x} \right)}}{{{x^2}}}} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}y = {\left( {x\cos x} \right)^x} + {\left( {x\sin x} \right)^{\frac{1}{x}}}{/tex}

Putting u = (x cos x)x and {tex}v = {\left( {x\sin x} \right)^{\frac{1}{x}}}{/tex}

we have y = u + v

{tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{du}}{{dx}} + \frac{{dv}}{{dx}}{/tex} ....(i)

Now u = (x cos x)x

{tex}\Rightarrow \log u = \log {\left( {x\cos x} \right)^x} = x\log \left( {x\cos x} \right){/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \log u = x\left( {\log x + \log \cos x} \right){/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d}{{dx}}\log u = \frac{d}{{dx}}\left\{ {x\left( {\log x + \log \cos x} \right)} \right\}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{u}\frac{{du}}{{dx}}{/tex} {tex} = x\left[ {\frac{1}{x} + \frac{1}{{\cos x}}.\left( { - \sin x} \right)} \right] + \left( {\log x + \log \cos x} \right).1{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{u}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} {/tex}{tex} = \left[ {1 - x\tan x + \log \left( {x\cos x} \right)} \right]{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{du}}{{dx}} = u\left[ {1 - x\tan x + \log \left( {x\cos x} \right)} \right]{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{du}}{{dx}} = {\left( {x\cos x} \right)^x}\left[ {1 - x\tan x + \log \left( {x\cos x} \right)} \right]{/tex} .....(ii)

Again {tex}v = {\left( {x\sin x} \right)^{\frac{1}{x}}}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \log v = \log {\left( {x\sin x} \right)^{\frac{1}{x}}} = \frac{1}{x}\log \left( {x\sin x} \right){/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \log v = \frac{1}{x}\left( {\log x + \log \sin x} \right){/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d}{{dx}}\log v = \frac{d}{{dx}}\left\{ {\frac{1}{x}\left( {\log x + \log \sin x} \right)} \right\}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}\frac{dv}{{dx}} {/tex}{tex}= \frac{1}{x}\left[ {\frac{1}{x} + \frac{1}{{\ sinx }}.\cos x} \right] + \left( {\log x + \log \sin x} \right)\left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{{{x^2}}}} \right){/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}\frac{dv}{{dx}} {/tex}{tex}= \left[ {\frac{1}{{{x^2}}} + \frac{{\cot x}}{x} - \frac{{\log \left( {x\sin x} \right)}}{{{x^2}}}} \right]{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{dv}{{dx}} = v\left[ {\frac{1}{{{x^2}}} + \frac{{\cot x}}{x} - \frac{{\log \left( {x\sin x} \right)}}{{{x^2}}}} \right]{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{dv}{{dx}} = {\left( {x\sin x} \right)^{\frac{1}{x}}}\left[ {\frac{1}{{{x^2}}} + \frac{{\cot x}}{x} - \frac{{\log \left( {x\sin x} \right)}}{{{x^2}}}} \right]{/tex} ...(iii)

Putting the values from eq. (ii) and (iii) in eq. (i)

{tex}\frac{d}{{dx}} = {\left( {x\cos x} \right)^{^x}}{/tex}{tex}\left[ {1 - x\tan x + \log \left( {x\cos x} \right)} \right] + {\left( {x\sin x} \right)^{\frac{1}{x}}}{/tex}{tex}\left[ {\frac{1}{{{x^2}}} + \frac{{\cot x}}{x} - \frac{{\log \left( {x\sin x} \right)}}{{{x^2}}}} \right]{/tex}

question_html: Differentiate the function (log x)x + xlog x w.r.t. x.
question_text: Differentiate the function (log x) x + x log x w.r.t. x.
explanation: Given: (log x) x + x log x Let y = (log x) x + x log x Let y = u + v {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} u = (log x) x and v = x log x For, u = (log x) x Taking log on both sides, we get log u = log (log x) x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = x.log (log (x)) Now, differentiate both sides with respect to x {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\log \mathrm{u})=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}[\mathrm{x} \cdot \log (\log \mathrm{x})]{/tex} {tex}\left.\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{u}} \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \log (\log \mathrm{x})\right)+\log (\log \mathrm{x}) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\mathrm{x}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{u}\left[\mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{1}{\log \mathrm{x}} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\log \mathrm{x})+\log (\log \mathrm{x}) \cdot(1)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{u}}{\mathrm{dx}}=(\log \mathrm{x})^{\mathrm{x}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\log \mathrm{x}} \cdot \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}+\log (\log \mathrm{x}) \cdot(1)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=(\log x)^{x}\left[\frac{1+\log (\log x) \cdot(\log x)}{\log x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=(\log x)^{x-1}[1+\log x \cdot \log (\log x)]{/tex} For, v = x log x Taking log on both sides, we get log v = log (x log x ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log v = log x. log x Now, differentiate both sides with respect to x {tex}\frac{d}{d x}(\log v)=\frac{d}{d x}\left[(\log x)^{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=2 \cdot \log \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\log \mathrm{x}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=v\left[2 \cdot \frac{\log x}{x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=x^{\log x}\left[2 \cdot \frac{\log x}{x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=2 \cdot x^{\log x-1} \cdot \log x{/tex} Because, y = u + v {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=(\log x)^{x-1}[1+\log x \cdot \log (\log x)]+2 \cdot x^{\log x-1} \cdot \log x{/tex}
solution: Given: (log x) x + x log x Let y = (log x) x + x log x Let y = u + v {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} u = (log x) x and v = x log x For, u = (log x) x Taking log on both sides, we get log u = log (log x) x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = x.log (log (x)) Now, differentiate both sides with respect to x {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\log \mathrm{u})=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}[\mathrm{x} \cdot \log (\log \mathrm{x})]{/tex} {tex}\left.\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{u}} \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \log (\log \mathrm{x})\right)+\log (\log \mathrm{x}) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\mathrm{x}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{u}\left[\mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{1}{\log \mathrm{x}} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\log \mathrm{x})+\log (\log \mathrm{x}) \cdot(1)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{u}}{\mathrm{dx}}=(\log \mathrm{x})^{\mathrm{x}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\log \mathrm{x}} \cdot \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}+\log (\log \mathrm{x}) \cdot(1)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=(\log x)^{x}\left[\frac{1+\log (\log x) \cdot(\log x)}{\log x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=(\log x)^{x-1}[1+\log x \cdot \log (\log x)]{/tex} For, v = x log x Taking log on both sides, we get log v = log (x log x ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log v = log x. log x Now, differentiate both sides with respect to x {tex}\frac{d}{d x}(\log v)=\frac{d}{d x}\left[(\log x)^{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=2 \cdot \log \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\log \mathrm{x}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=v\left[2 \cdot \frac{\log x}{x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=x^{\log x}\left[2 \cdot \frac{\log x}{x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=2 \cdot x^{\log x-1} \cdot \log x{/tex} Because, y = u + v {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=(\log x)^{x-1}[1+\log x \cdot \log (\log x)]+2 \cdot x^{\log x-1} \cdot \log x{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: (log x)x + xlog x 
Let y = (log x)x + xlog x 
Let y = u + v
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} u = (log x)x and v = xlog x 
For, u = (log x)x 
Taking log on both sides, we get
log u = log (log x)x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = x.log (log (x))
Now, differentiate both sides with respect to x
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\log \mathrm{u})=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}[\mathrm{x} \cdot \log (\log \mathrm{x})]{/tex} 
{tex}\left.\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{u}} \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \log (\log \mathrm{x})\right)+\log (\log \mathrm{x}) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\mathrm{x}){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{u}\left[\mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{1}{\log \mathrm{x}} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\log \mathrm{x})+\log (\log \mathrm{x}) \cdot(1)\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{u}}{\mathrm{dx}}=(\log \mathrm{x})^{\mathrm{x}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\log \mathrm{x}} \cdot \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}+\log (\log \mathrm{x}) \cdot(1)\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=(\log x)^{x}\left[\frac{1+\log (\log x) \cdot(\log x)}{\log x}\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=(\log x)^{x-1}[1+\log x \cdot \log (\log x)]{/tex} 
For, v = xlog x  
Taking log on both sides, we get
log v = log (xlog x)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log v = log x. log x
Now, differentiate both sides with respect to x
{tex}\frac{d}{d x}(\log v)=\frac{d}{d x}\left[(\log x)^{2}\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=2 \cdot \log \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\log \mathrm{x}){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=v\left[2 \cdot \frac{\log x}{x}\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=x^{\log x}\left[2 \cdot \frac{\log x}{x}\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=2 \cdot x^{\log x-1} \cdot \log x{/tex} 
Because, y = u + v
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=(\log x)^{x-1}[1+\log x \cdot \log (\log x)]+2 \cdot x^{\log x-1} \cdot \log x{/tex}

question_html: Differentiate the function {tex}\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{x}+x^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}{/tex} w.r.t. x.
question_text: Differentiate the function {tex}\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{x}+x^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}{/tex} w.r.t. x.
explanation: Given: {tex}\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{x}+x^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}{/tex} Let y = {tex}\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{x}+x^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}{/tex} Also, let y = u + v {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{u}=\left(\mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)^x{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{x}^{\left(1+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)}{/tex} For, {tex}\mathrm{u}=\left(\mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)^{\mathrm{x}}{/tex} Taking log on both sides, we get {tex}\log u=\log \left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{x}{/tex} Now, differentiate both sides with respect to x {tex}\frac{d}{d x}(\log u)=\frac{d}{d x}\left[x \cdot \log \left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{u}} \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\log \left(\mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)\right)+\log \left(\mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\mathrm{x}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{u}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{u}\left[\mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{1}{\left(\mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)+\log \left(\mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{u}\left[\mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{1}{\left(\mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)} \cdot\left(\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dx}}+\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)\right)+\log \left(\mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=u\left[\frac{x}{\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)} \cdot\left(1-\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)+\log \left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=u\left[\frac{x}{\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)} \cdot\left(\frac{x^{2}-1}{x^{2}}\right)+\log \left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{x}\left[\left(\frac{x^{2}-1}{x^{2}+1}\right)+\log \left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)\right]{/tex} For, {tex}\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{x}^{\left(1+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)}{/tex} Taking log on both sides, we get {tex}\log v=\log {x}^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log \mathrm{v}=\left(1+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right) \cdot \log \mathrm{x}{/tex} Now, differentiate both sides with respect to x {tex}\frac{d}{d x}(\log v)=\frac{d}{d x}\left[\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right) \cdot \log x\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\log \mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(1+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)+\left(1+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\log \mathrm{x}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d V}{d x}=v\left[\log x \cdot\left(0-\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)+\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=x^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}\left[-\frac{\log x}{x^{2}}+\left(\frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=x^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}\left[\frac{-\log x+x+1}{x^{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=x^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}\left[\frac{x+1-\log x}{x^{2}}\right]{/tex} Because, y = u + v {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{x}\left[\left(\frac{x^{2}-1}{x^{2}+1}\right)+\log \left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)\right]+x^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}\left[\frac{x+1-\log x}{x^{2}}\right]{/tex}
solution: Given: {tex}\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{x}+x^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}{/tex} Let y = {tex}\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{x}+x^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}{/tex} Also, let y = u + v {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{u}=\left(\mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)^x{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{x}^{\left(1+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)}{/tex} For, {tex}\mathrm{u}=\left(\mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)^{\mathrm{x}}{/tex} Taking log on both sides, we get {tex}\log u=\log \left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{x}{/tex} Now, differentiate both sides with respect to x {tex}\frac{d}{d x}(\log u)=\frac{d}{d x}\left[x \cdot \log \left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{u}} \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\log \left(\mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)\right)+\log \left(\mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\mathrm{x}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{u}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{u}\left[\mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{1}{\left(\mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)+\log \left(\mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{u}\left[\mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{1}{\left(\mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)} \cdot\left(\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dx}}+\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)\right)+\log \left(\mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=u\left[\frac{x}{\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)} \cdot\left(1-\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)+\log \left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=u\left[\frac{x}{\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)} \cdot\left(\frac{x^{2}-1}{x^{2}}\right)+\log \left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{x}\left[\left(\frac{x^{2}-1}{x^{2}+1}\right)+\log \left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)\right]{/tex} For, {tex}\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{x}^{\left(1+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)}{/tex} Taking log on both sides, we get {tex}\log v=\log {x}^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log \mathrm{v}=\left(1+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right) \cdot \log \mathrm{x}{/tex} Now, differentiate both sides with respect to x {tex}\frac{d}{d x}(\log v)=\frac{d}{d x}\left[\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right) \cdot \log x\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\log \mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(1+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)+\left(1+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\log \mathrm{x}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d V}{d x}=v\left[\log x \cdot\left(0-\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)+\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=x^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}\left[-\frac{\log x}{x^{2}}+\left(\frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=x^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}\left[\frac{-\log x+x+1}{x^{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=x^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}\left[\frac{x+1-\log x}{x^{2}}\right]{/tex} Because, y = u + v {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{x}\left[\left(\frac{x^{2}-1}{x^{2}+1}\right)+\log \left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)\right]+x^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}\left[\frac{x+1-\log x}{x^{2}}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: {tex}\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{x}+x^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}{/tex}
Let y = {tex}\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{x}+x^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}{/tex}
Also, let y = u + v
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{u}=\left(\mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)^x{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{x}^{\left(1+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)}{/tex}
For, {tex}\mathrm{u}=\left(\mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)^{\mathrm{x}}{/tex} 
Taking log on both sides, we get
{tex}\log u=\log \left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{x}{/tex} 
Now, differentiate both sides with respect to x
{tex}\frac{d}{d x}(\log u)=\frac{d}{d x}\left[x \cdot \log \left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{u}} \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\log \left(\mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)\right)+\log \left(\mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\mathrm{x}){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{u}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{u}\left[\mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{1}{\left(\mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)+\log \left(\mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{u}\left[\mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{1}{\left(\mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)} \cdot\left(\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dx}}+\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)\right)+\log \left(\mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=u\left[\frac{x}{\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)} \cdot\left(1-\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)+\log \left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=u\left[\frac{x}{\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)} \cdot\left(\frac{x^{2}-1}{x^{2}}\right)+\log \left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{x}\left[\left(\frac{x^{2}-1}{x^{2}+1}\right)+\log \left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)\right]{/tex}
For, {tex}\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{x}^{\left(1+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)}{/tex} 
Taking log on both sides, we get
{tex}\log v=\log {x}^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \log \mathrm{v}=\left(1+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right) \cdot \log \mathrm{x}{/tex} 
Now, differentiate both sides with respect to x
{tex}\frac{d}{d x}(\log v)=\frac{d}{d x}\left[\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right) \cdot \log x\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\log \mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(1+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)+\left(1+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\log \mathrm{x}){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d V}{d x}=v\left[\log x \cdot\left(0-\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)+\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{x}\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=x^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}\left[-\frac{\log x}{x^{2}}+\left(\frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=x^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}\left[\frac{-\log x+x+1}{x^{2}}\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=x^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}\left[\frac{x+1-\log x}{x^{2}}\right]{/tex} 
Because, y = u + v
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{x}\left[\left(\frac{x^{2}-1}{x^{2}+1}\right)+\log \left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)\right]+x^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}\left[\frac{x+1-\log x}{x^{2}}\right]{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} + \sqrt {1 - {y^2}} = a\left( {x - y} \right){/tex}, prove that {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \sqrt {\frac{{1 - {y^2}}}{{1 - {x^2}}}} {/tex}
question_text: If {tex}\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} + \sqrt {1 - {y^2}} = a\left( {x - y} \right){/tex} , prove that {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \sqrt {\frac{{1 - {y^2}}}{{1 - {x^2}}}} {/tex}
explanation: We have, {tex}\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} + \sqrt {1 - {y^2}} = a\left( {x - y} \right){/tex} On putting {tex}x = \sin \alpha {/tex} and {tex}y = \sin \beta {/tex} we get {tex}\sqrt {1 - {{\sin }^2}\alpha } + \sqrt {1 - {{\sin }^2}\beta } = a\left( {\sin \alpha - \sin \beta } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos \alpha + \cos \beta = a\left( {\sin \alpha - \sin \beta } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2\cos \frac{{\alpha + \beta }}{2}.\cos \frac{{\alpha - \beta }}{2} = a\left( {2\cos \frac{{\alpha + \beta }}{2}.\sin \frac{{\alpha - \beta }}{2}} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos \frac{{\alpha - \beta }}{2} = a\sin \frac{{\alpha - \beta }}{2}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \cot \frac{{\alpha - \beta }}{2} = a{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{\alpha - \beta }}{2} = {\cot ^{ - 1}}a{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \alpha - \beta = 2{\cot ^{ - 1}}a{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {\sin ^{ - 1}}x - {\sin ^{ - 1}}y = 2{\cot ^{ - 1}}a\,\left[ {\because x = \sin \alpha \,and\,y = \sin \beta } \right]{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{1}{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }} - \frac{1}{{\sqrt {1 - {y^2}} }}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{\sqrt {1 - {y^2}} }}{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }} {/tex} . Hence proved.
solution: We have, {tex}\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} + \sqrt {1 - {y^2}} = a\left( {x - y} \right){/tex} On putting {tex}x = \sin \alpha {/tex} and {tex}y = \sin \beta {/tex} we get {tex}\sqrt {1 - {{\sin }^2}\alpha } + \sqrt {1 - {{\sin }^2}\beta } = a\left( {\sin \alpha - \sin \beta } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos \alpha + \cos \beta = a\left( {\sin \alpha - \sin \beta } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2\cos \frac{{\alpha + \beta }}{2}.\cos \frac{{\alpha - \beta }}{2} = a\left( {2\cos \frac{{\alpha + \beta }}{2}.\sin \frac{{\alpha - \beta }}{2}} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos \frac{{\alpha - \beta }}{2} = a\sin \frac{{\alpha - \beta }}{2}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \cot \frac{{\alpha - \beta }}{2} = a{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{\alpha - \beta }}{2} = {\cot ^{ - 1}}a{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \alpha - \beta = 2{\cot ^{ - 1}}a{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {\sin ^{ - 1}}x - {\sin ^{ - 1}}y = 2{\cot ^{ - 1}}a\,\left[ {\because x = \sin \alpha \,and\,y = \sin \beta } \right]{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{1}{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }} - \frac{1}{{\sqrt {1 - {y^2}} }}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{\sqrt {1 - {y^2}} }}{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }} {/tex} . Hence proved.
solution_html:
We have,
{tex}\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} + \sqrt {1 - {y^2}} = a\left( {x - y} \right){/tex}
On putting {tex}x = \sin \alpha {/tex} and {tex}y = \sin \beta {/tex} we get
{tex}\sqrt {1 - {{\sin }^2}\alpha } + \sqrt {1 - {{\sin }^2}\beta } = a\left( {\sin \alpha - \sin \beta } \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \cos \alpha + \cos \beta = a\left( {\sin \alpha - \sin \beta } \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2\cos \frac{{\alpha + \beta }}{2}.\cos \frac{{\alpha - \beta }}{2} = a\left( {2\cos \frac{{\alpha + \beta }}{2}.\sin \frac{{\alpha - \beta }}{2}} \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \cos \frac{{\alpha - \beta }}{2} = a\sin \frac{{\alpha - \beta }}{2}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \cot \frac{{\alpha - \beta }}{2} = a{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{\alpha - \beta }}{2} = {\cot ^{ - 1}}a{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \alpha - \beta = 2{\cot ^{ - 1}}a{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {\sin ^{ - 1}}x - {\sin ^{ - 1}}y = 2{\cot ^{ - 1}}a\,\left[ {\because x = \sin \alpha \,and\,y = \sin \beta } \right]{/tex}
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac{1}{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }} - \frac{1}{{\sqrt {1 - {y^2}} }}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{\sqrt {1 - {y^2}} }}{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }} {/tex}.
Hence proved.

question_text: If x = sin {tex}\left(\frac{1}{a} \log y\right){/tex} , show that (1 - x 2 ) y 2 - xy 1 - a 2 y = 0
explanation: Given, x = {tex}\sin \left(\frac{1}{a} \log y\right){/tex} (log y) = a sin -1 x {tex}y=e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}{/tex} ...(i) To prove: (1 - x 2 ) y 2 - xy 1 - a 2 y = 0 We notice a second-order derivative in the expression to be proved so first take the step to find the second-order derivative. Let's find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} As, {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} So, lets first find dy/dx {tex}\because y=e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}{/tex} Let t = asin -1 x {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d t}{d x}=\frac{a}{\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}}{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \sin ^{-1} \mathrm{x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\left(1-\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)}}\right]{/tex} And y = e t {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y}{d t} \frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=e^{t} \frac{a}{\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}}=\frac{a e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}}{\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}}{/tex} ...(ii) Again differentiating with respect to x applying product rule: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=\operatorname{ae}^{\operatorname{asin}^{-1} \mathrm{x}} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}^{2}}}\right)+\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\sqrt{\left(1-\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)}} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{asin}^{-1} \mathrm{x}}{/tex} Using chain rule and equation (ii) {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=-\frac{\operatorname{ae}^{a \sin ^{-1} x}}{2\left(1-x^{2}\right) \sqrt{1-x^{2}}}(-2 x)+\frac{a^{2} e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}{/tex} [using {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left(x^{n}\right)=n x^{n-1} \frac{d}{d x} \sin ^{-1} x=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}}{/tex} ] {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{x a e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}}{\left(1-x^{2}\right) \sqrt{1-x^{2}}}+\frac{a^{2} e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\left(1-\mathrm{x}^{2}\right) \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=\mathrm{a}^{2} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{asin}^{-1} \mathrm{x}}+\frac{\mathrm{xae}^{\mathrm{asin}^{-1} \mathrm{x}}}{\sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}^{2}}}{/tex} Using equation (i) and equation (ii) {tex}\left(1-\mathrm{x}^{2}\right) \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=\mathrm{a}^{2} \mathrm{y}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} (1 - x 2 ) y 2 - xy 1 - a 2 y = 0 Hence proved.
solution: Given, x = {tex}\sin \left(\frac{1}{a} \log y\right){/tex} (log y) = a sin -1 x {tex}y=e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}{/tex} ...(i) To prove: (1 - x 2 ) y 2 - xy 1 - a 2 y = 0 We notice a second-order derivative in the expression to be proved so first take the step to find the second-order derivative. Let's find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} As, {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} So, lets first find dy/dx {tex}\because y=e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}{/tex} Let t = asin -1 x {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d t}{d x}=\frac{a}{\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}}{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \sin ^{-1} \mathrm{x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\left(1-\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)}}\right]{/tex} And y = e t {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y}{d t} \frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=e^{t} \frac{a}{\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}}=\frac{a e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}}{\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}}{/tex} ...(ii) Again differentiating with respect to x applying product rule: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=\operatorname{ae}^{\operatorname{asin}^{-1} \mathrm{x}} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}^{2}}}\right)+\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\sqrt{\left(1-\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)}} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{asin}^{-1} \mathrm{x}}{/tex} Using chain rule and equation (ii) {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=-\frac{\operatorname{ae}^{a \sin ^{-1} x}}{2\left(1-x^{2}\right) \sqrt{1-x^{2}}}(-2 x)+\frac{a^{2} e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}{/tex} [using {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left(x^{n}\right)=n x^{n-1} \frac{d}{d x} \sin ^{-1} x=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}}{/tex} ] {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{x a e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}}{\left(1-x^{2}\right) \sqrt{1-x^{2}}}+\frac{a^{2} e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\left(1-\mathrm{x}^{2}\right) \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=\mathrm{a}^{2} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{asin}^{-1} \mathrm{x}}+\frac{\mathrm{xae}^{\mathrm{asin}^{-1} \mathrm{x}}}{\sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}^{2}}}{/tex} Using equation (i) and equation (ii) {tex}\left(1-\mathrm{x}^{2}\right) \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=\mathrm{a}^{2} \mathrm{y}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} (1 - x 2 ) y 2 - xy 1 - a 2 y = 0 Hence proved.
solution_html:
Given,
x = {tex}\sin \left(\frac{1}{a} \log y\right){/tex}
(log y) = a sin-1x
{tex}y=e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}{/tex} ...(i)
To prove: (1 - x2) y2 - xy1 - a2y = 0
We notice a second-order derivative in the expression to be proved so first take the step to find the second-order derivative.
Let's find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex}
As, {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex}
So, lets first find dy/dx
{tex}\because y=e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}{/tex}
Let t = asin-1x
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d t}{d x}=\frac{a}{\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}}{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \sin ^{-1} \mathrm{x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\left(1-\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)}}\right]{/tex}
And y = et
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y}{d t} \frac{d t}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=e^{t} \frac{a}{\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}}=\frac{a e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}}{\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}}{/tex} ...(ii)
Again differentiating with respect to x applying product rule:
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=\operatorname{ae}^{\operatorname{asin}^{-1} \mathrm{x}} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}^{2}}}\right)+\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\sqrt{\left(1-\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)}} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{asin}^{-1} \mathrm{x}}{/tex}
Using chain rule and equation (ii)
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=-\frac{\operatorname{ae}^{a \sin ^{-1} x}}{2\left(1-x^{2}\right) \sqrt{1-x^{2}}}(-2 x)+\frac{a^{2} e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}{/tex}
[using {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left(x^{n}\right)=n x^{n-1} \frac{d}{d x} \sin ^{-1} x=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}}{/tex}]
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{x a e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}}{\left(1-x^{2}\right) \sqrt{1-x^{2}}}+\frac{a^{2} e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\left(1-\mathrm{x}^{2}\right) \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=\mathrm{a}^{2} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{asin}^{-1} \mathrm{x}}+\frac{\mathrm{xae}^{\mathrm{asin}^{-1} \mathrm{x}}}{\sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}^{2}}}{/tex}
Using equation (i) and equation (ii)
{tex}\left(1-\mathrm{x}^{2}\right) \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=\mathrm{a}^{2} \mathrm{y}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (1 - x2) y2 - xy1 - a2y = 0
Hence proved.

question_html: Find {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} if {tex}{y^x} + {x^y} + {x^x} = {a^b}{/tex}
question_text: Find {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} if {tex}{y^x} + {x^y} + {x^x} = {a^b}{/tex}
explanation: Let {tex}u = {y^x},v = {x^y},w = {x^x}{/tex} {tex}u + v + w = {a^b}{/tex} Therefore {tex}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} + \frac{{dw}}{{dx}} + \frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} ....(1) {tex}u = {y^x}{/tex} Taking log both side {tex}\log u = \log {y^x}{/tex} {tex}\log u = x.\log y{/tex} Differentiate both side w.r.t. to x {tex}\frac{1}{u}.\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = x.\frac{1}{y}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \log y.1{/tex} {tex}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = u\left[ {\frac{x}{y}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \log y} \right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = {y^x}\left[ {\frac{x}{y}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \log y} \right]{/tex} .... (2) {tex}v = {x^y}{/tex} Taking log both side {tex}\log v = \log {x^y}{/tex} {tex}\log v = y.\log x{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}.\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = y.\frac{1}{x} + \log x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = v\left[ {\frac{y}{x} + \log x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = {x^y}\left[ {\frac{y}{x} + \log x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right]{/tex} .... (3) {tex}w = {x^x}{/tex} Taking log both side {tex}\log w = \log {x^x}{/tex} {tex}\log w = x\log x{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{w}.\frac{{dw}}{{dx}} = x.\frac{1}{x} + \log x.1{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{w}.\frac{{dw}}{{dx}} = 1 + \log x{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dw}}{{dx}} = w(1 + \log x){/tex} {tex}\frac{{dw}}{{dx}} = {x^x}(1 + \log x){/tex} .... (4) {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{ - {x^x}(1 + \log x) - y.{x^{y - 1}} - {y^x}\log y}}{{x.{y^{x - 1}} + {x^y}\log x.}}{/tex} (by putting 2,3 and 4 in 1)
solution: Let {tex}u = {y^x},v = {x^y},w = {x^x}{/tex} {tex}u + v + w = {a^b}{/tex} Therefore {tex}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} + \frac{{dw}}{{dx}} + \frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} ....(1) {tex}u = {y^x}{/tex} Taking log both side {tex}\log u = \log {y^x}{/tex} {tex}\log u = x.\log y{/tex} Differentiate both side w.r.t. to x {tex}\frac{1}{u}.\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = x.\frac{1}{y}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \log y.1{/tex} {tex}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = u\left[ {\frac{x}{y}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \log y} \right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = {y^x}\left[ {\frac{x}{y}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \log y} \right]{/tex} .... (2) {tex}v = {x^y}{/tex} Taking log both side {tex}\log v = \log {x^y}{/tex} {tex}\log v = y.\log x{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}.\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = y.\frac{1}{x} + \log x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = v\left[ {\frac{y}{x} + \log x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = {x^y}\left[ {\frac{y}{x} + \log x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right]{/tex} .... (3) {tex}w = {x^x}{/tex} Taking log both side {tex}\log w = \log {x^x}{/tex} {tex}\log w = x\log x{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{w}.\frac{{dw}}{{dx}} = x.\frac{1}{x} + \log x.1{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{w}.\frac{{dw}}{{dx}} = 1 + \log x{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dw}}{{dx}} = w(1 + \log x){/tex} {tex}\frac{{dw}}{{dx}} = {x^x}(1 + \log x){/tex} .... (4) {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{ - {x^x}(1 + \log x) - y.{x^{y - 1}} - {y^x}\log y}}{{x.{y^{x - 1}} + {x^y}\log x.}}{/tex} (by putting 2,3 and 4 in 1)
solution_html:
Let {tex}u = {y^x},v = {x^y},w = {x^x}{/tex}

{tex}u + v + w = {a^b}{/tex}

Therefore {tex}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} + \frac{{dw}}{{dx}} + \frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} ....(1)

{tex}u = {y^x}{/tex}

Taking log both side

{tex}\log u = \log {y^x}{/tex}

{tex}\log u = x.\log y{/tex}

Differentiate both side w.r.t. to x

{tex}\frac{1}{u}.\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = x.\frac{1}{y}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \log y.1{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = u\left[ {\frac{x}{y}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \log y} \right]{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = {y^x}\left[ {\frac{x}{y}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \log y} \right]{/tex}.... (2)

{tex}v = {x^y}{/tex}

Taking log both side

{tex}\log v = \log {x^y}{/tex}

{tex}\log v = y.\log x{/tex}

{tex}\frac{1}{v}.\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = y.\frac{1}{x} + \log x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = v\left[ {\frac{y}{x} + \log x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right]{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = {x^y}\left[ {\frac{y}{x} + \log x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right]{/tex}.... (3)

{tex}w = {x^x}{/tex}

Taking log both side

{tex}\log w = \log {x^x}{/tex}

{tex}\log w = x\log x{/tex}

{tex}\frac{1}{w}.\frac{{dw}}{{dx}} = x.\frac{1}{x} + \log x.1{/tex}

{tex}\frac{1}{w}.\frac{{dw}}{{dx}} = 1 + \log x{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{dw}}{{dx}} = w(1 + \log x){/tex}

{tex}\frac{{dw}}{{dx}} = {x^x}(1 + \log x){/tex}.... (4)

{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{ - {x^x}(1 + \log x) - y.{x^{y - 1}} - {y^x}\log y}}{{x.{y^{x - 1}} + {x^y}\log x.}}{/tex} (by putting 2,3 and 4 in 1)

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} of the function xy + yx = 1
question_text: Find {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} of the function x y + y x = 1
explanation: Given: x y + y x = 1 Let y = x y + y x = 1 Let u = x y and v = y x Then, u + v = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}=0{/tex} For, u = x y Taking log on both sides, we get log u = log x y {tex}\Rightarrow \log u=y \cdot \log (x){/tex} Now, differentiating both sides with respect to x {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\log \mathrm{u})=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}[\mathrm{y} \cdot \log (\mathrm{x})]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{u}} \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\left\{\mathrm{y} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\log \mathrm{x})+\log \mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\mathrm{y})\right\}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=u\left[y \cdot \frac{1}{x}+\log x \cdot\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{y}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}+\log \mathrm{x} \cdot\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right)\right]{/tex} For v = y x Taking log on both sides, we get log v = log y x {tex}\Rightarrow \log v=x \cdot \log (y){/tex} Now, differentiate both sides with respect to x {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\log \mathrm{v})=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}[\mathrm{x} \cdot \log (\mathrm{y})]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\left\{\mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\log \mathrm{y})+\log \mathrm{y} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \mathrm{x}\right\}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=v\left[x \cdot \frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}+\log y \cdot\left(\frac{d x}{d x}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=y^{x}\left[\frac{x}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}+\log y\right]{/tex} because, {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}=0{/tex} So, {tex}\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{y}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}+\log \mathrm{x} \cdot\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right)\right]+\mathrm{y}^{\mathrm{x}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{y}} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+\log \mathrm{y}\right]=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left(\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{y}} \log \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{xy}^{\mathrm{x}-1}\right) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+\left(\mathrm{yx}^{\mathrm{y}-1}+\mathrm{y}^{\mathrm{x}} \log \mathrm{y}\right)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left(\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{y}} \log \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{xy}^{\mathrm{x}-1}\right) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=-\left(\mathrm{yx}^{\mathrm{y}-1}+\mathrm{y}^{\mathrm{x}} \log \mathrm{y}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{\left(y x^{y-1}+y^{x} \log y\right)}{\left(x^{y} \log x+x y^{x-1}\right)}{/tex}
solution: Given: x y + y x = 1 Let y = x y + y x = 1 Let u = x y and v = y x Then, u + v = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}=0{/tex} For, u = x y Taking log on both sides, we get log u = log x y {tex}\Rightarrow \log u=y \cdot \log (x){/tex} Now, differentiating both sides with respect to x {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\log \mathrm{u})=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}[\mathrm{y} \cdot \log (\mathrm{x})]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{u}} \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\left\{\mathrm{y} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\log \mathrm{x})+\log \mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\mathrm{y})\right\}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=u\left[y \cdot \frac{1}{x}+\log x \cdot\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{y}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}+\log \mathrm{x} \cdot\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right)\right]{/tex} For v = y x Taking log on both sides, we get log v = log y x {tex}\Rightarrow \log v=x \cdot \log (y){/tex} Now, differentiate both sides with respect to x {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\log \mathrm{v})=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}[\mathrm{x} \cdot \log (\mathrm{y})]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\left\{\mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\log \mathrm{y})+\log \mathrm{y} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \mathrm{x}\right\}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=v\left[x \cdot \frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}+\log y \cdot\left(\frac{d x}{d x}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=y^{x}\left[\frac{x}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}+\log y\right]{/tex} because, {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}=0{/tex} So, {tex}\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{y}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}+\log \mathrm{x} \cdot\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right)\right]+\mathrm{y}^{\mathrm{x}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{y}} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+\log \mathrm{y}\right]=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left(\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{y}} \log \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{xy}^{\mathrm{x}-1}\right) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+\left(\mathrm{yx}^{\mathrm{y}-1}+\mathrm{y}^{\mathrm{x}} \log \mathrm{y}\right)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left(\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{y}} \log \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{xy}^{\mathrm{x}-1}\right) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=-\left(\mathrm{yx}^{\mathrm{y}-1}+\mathrm{y}^{\mathrm{x}} \log \mathrm{y}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{\left(y x^{y-1}+y^{x} \log y\right)}{\left(x^{y} \log x+x y^{x-1}\right)}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: xy + yx = 1
Let y = xy + yx = 1
Let u = xy and v = yx
Then, u + v = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}=0{/tex} 
For, u = xy 
Taking log on both sides, we get
log u = log xy 
{tex}\Rightarrow \log u=y \cdot \log (x){/tex} 
Now, differentiating both sides with respect to x
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\log \mathrm{u})=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}[\mathrm{y} \cdot \log (\mathrm{x})]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{u}} \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\left\{\mathrm{y} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\log \mathrm{x})+\log \mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\mathrm{y})\right\}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=u\left[y \cdot \frac{1}{x}+\log x \cdot\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{y}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}+\log \mathrm{x} \cdot\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right)\right]{/tex} 
For v = yx 
Taking log on both sides, we get
log v = log yx  
{tex}\Rightarrow \log v=x \cdot \log (y){/tex} 
Now, differentiate both sides with respect to x
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\log \mathrm{v})=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}[\mathrm{x} \cdot \log (\mathrm{y})]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\left\{\mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\log \mathrm{y})+\log \mathrm{y} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \mathrm{x}\right\}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=v\left[x \cdot \frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}+\log y \cdot\left(\frac{d x}{d x}\right)\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=y^{x}\left[\frac{x}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}+\log y\right]{/tex} 
because, {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}=0{/tex} 
So, {tex}\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{y}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}+\log \mathrm{x} \cdot\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right)\right]+\mathrm{y}^{\mathrm{x}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{y}} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+\log \mathrm{y}\right]=0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{y}} \log \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{xy}^{\mathrm{x}-1}\right) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+\left(\mathrm{yx}^{\mathrm{y}-1}+\mathrm{y}^{\mathrm{x}} \log \mathrm{y}\right)=0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{y}} \log \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{xy}^{\mathrm{x}-1}\right) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=-\left(\mathrm{yx}^{\mathrm{y}-1}+\mathrm{y}^{\mathrm{x}} \log \mathrm{y}\right){/tex} 
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{\left(y x^{y-1}+y^{x} \log y\right)}{\left(x^{y} \log x+x y^{x-1}\right)}{/tex}
