question_html: If {tex}x\sqrt {1 - y} + y\sqrt {1 + x} = 0{/tex} prove that {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \frac{1}{{{{(1 + x)}^2}}}{/tex}
question_text: If {tex}x\sqrt {1 - y} + y\sqrt {1 + x} = 0{/tex} prove that {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \frac{1}{{{{(1 + x)}^2}}}{/tex}
explanation: {tex}x\sqrt {1 + y} = - y\sqrt {1 + x} {/tex} Squaring both sides {tex}{x^2}(1 + y) = {y^2}(1 + x){/tex} {tex}{x^2} + {x^2}y = {y^2} + x{y^2}{/tex} {tex}{x^2} - {y^2} + {x^2}y - x{y^2} = 0{/tex} {tex}(x - y)(x + y) + xy(x - y) = 0{/tex} {tex}(x - y)[x + y + xy] = 0{/tex} {tex}x + y + xy = 0{/tex} {tex}y(1 + x) = - x{/tex} {tex}y = \frac{{ - x}}{{1 + x}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \left[ {\frac{{(1 + x)(1) - (x)(1)}}{{{{(1 + x)}^2}}}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = - \left[ {\frac{{1 + x - x}}{{{{(1 + x)}^2}}}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{ - 1}}{{{{(1 + x)}^2}}}{/tex}
solution: {tex}x\sqrt {1 + y} = - y\sqrt {1 + x} {/tex} Squaring both sides {tex}{x^2}(1 + y) = {y^2}(1 + x){/tex} {tex}{x^2} + {x^2}y = {y^2} + x{y^2}{/tex} {tex}{x^2} - {y^2} + {x^2}y - x{y^2} = 0{/tex} {tex}(x - y)(x + y) + xy(x - y) = 0{/tex} {tex}(x - y)[x + y + xy] = 0{/tex} {tex}x + y + xy = 0{/tex} {tex}y(1 + x) = - x{/tex} {tex}y = \frac{{ - x}}{{1 + x}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \left[ {\frac{{(1 + x)(1) - (x)(1)}}{{{{(1 + x)}^2}}}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = - \left[ {\frac{{1 + x - x}}{{{{(1 + x)}^2}}}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{ - 1}}{{{{(1 + x)}^2}}}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}x\sqrt {1 + y} = - y\sqrt {1 + x} {/tex}

Squaring both sides

{tex}{x^2}(1 + y) = {y^2}(1 + x){/tex}

{tex}{x^2} + {x^2}y = {y^2} + x{y^2}{/tex}

{tex}{x^2} - {y^2} + {x^2}y - x{y^2} = 0{/tex}

{tex}(x - y)(x + y) + xy(x - y) = 0{/tex}

{tex}(x - y)[x + y + xy] = 0{/tex}

{tex}x + y + xy = 0{/tex}

{tex}y(1 + x) = - x{/tex}

{tex}y = \frac{{ - x}}{{1 + x}}{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \left[ {\frac{{(1 + x)(1) - (x)(1)}}{{{{(1 + x)}^2}}}} \right]{/tex}

{tex} = - \left[ {\frac{{1 + x - x}}{{{{(1 + x)}^2}}}} \right]{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{ - 1}}{{{{(1 + x)}^2}}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find all the points of discontinuity of f defined by f(x) = |x| – |x + 1|.
question_text: Find all the points of discontinuity of f defined by f(x) = |x| – |x + 1|.
explanation: It is given that f(x) = |x| - |x + 1| The given function f is defined for real number and f can be written as the composition of two functions, as f = goh, where g(x) = |x| and h(x) = |x + 1| Then, f = g - h First we have to prove that g(x) = |x| and h(x) = |x + 1| are continuous functions. g(x) = |x| can be written as {tex}g(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} {-x, \text { if } x<0} \\ {x, \text { if } x \geq 0} \end{array}\right.{/tex} Now, g is defined for all real number. Let k be a real number. Case I: If k < 0, Then g(k) = -k And {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} g(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} ( - x) = - k{/tex} Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} g(x) = g(k){/tex} Therefore, g is continuous at all points x, i.e., x > 0| Case II: If k > 0, Then g(k) = k and {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} g(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} x = k{/tex} Thus {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to {\mathbf{k}}} {\text{g}}({\text{x}}) = {\text{g}}({\text{k}}){/tex} Therefore, g is continuous at all points x, i.e., x < 0. Case III: If k = 0, Then, g(k) = g(0) = 0 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} g(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} ( - x) = 0{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} g(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} (x) = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} g(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} g(x) = g(0){/tex} Therefore, g is continuous at x = 0 From the above 3 cases, we get that g is continuous at all points. g(x) = |x + 1| can be written as {tex}g(x) = \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - (x + 1),{\text{ if }}x < - 1} \\ {x + 1,{\text{ if }}x \geqslant - 1} \end{array}} \right.{/tex} Now, h is defined for all real number. Let k be a real number. Case I: If k < -1, Then h(k) = -(k + 1) And {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} {\text{h}}({\text{x}}) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\text{x}} \to {\text{k}}} [ - ({\text{x}} + 1)] = - ({\text{k}} + 1){/tex} Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to {\mathbf{k}}} {\text{h}}({\text{x}}) = {\text{h}}({\text{k}}){/tex} Therefore, h is continuous at all points x, i.e., x < -1 Case II : If k > -1, Then h(k) = k + 1 and {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} h(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} (x + 1) = k + 1{/tex} Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} h(x) = h(k){/tex} Therefore, h is continuous at all points x, i.e., x > -1. Case III: If k = -1, Then, h(k) = h(-1) = -1 + 1 = 0 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} h(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} {/tex} [-(x + 1)] = -(-1 + 1) = 0 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} h(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} (x + 1){/tex} = -(-1 + 1) = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} h(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} h(x) = h( - 1){/tex} Therefore, g is continuous at x = -1 From the above 3 cases, we get that h is continuous at all points. Hence, g and h are continuous function. Therefore, f = g – h is also a continuous function.
solution: It is given that f(x) = |x| - |x + 1| The given function f is defined for real number and f can be written as the composition of two functions, as f = goh, where g(x) = |x| and h(x) = |x + 1| Then, f = g - h First we have to prove that g(x) = |x| and h(x) = |x + 1| are continuous functions. g(x) = |x| can be written as {tex}g(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} {-x, \text { if } x<0} \\ {x, \text { if } x \geq 0} \end{array}\right.{/tex} Now, g is defined for all real number. Let k be a real number. Case I: If k < 0, Then g(k) = -k And {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} g(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} ( - x) = - k{/tex} Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} g(x) = g(k){/tex} Therefore, g is continuous at all points x, i.e., x > 0| Case II: If k > 0, Then g(k) = k and {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} g(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} x = k{/tex} Thus {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to {\mathbf{k}}} {\text{g}}({\text{x}}) = {\text{g}}({\text{k}}){/tex} Therefore, g is continuous at all points x, i.e., x < 0. Case III: If k = 0, Then, g(k) = g(0) = 0 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} g(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} ( - x) = 0{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} g(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} (x) = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} g(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} g(x) = g(0){/tex} Therefore, g is continuous at x = 0 From the above 3 cases, we get that g is continuous at all points. g(x) = |x + 1| can be written as {tex}g(x) = \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - (x + 1),{\text{ if }}x < - 1} \\ {x + 1,{\text{ if }}x \geqslant - 1} \end{array}} \right.{/tex} Now, h is defined for all real number. Let k be a real number. Case I: If k < -1, Then h(k) = -(k + 1) And {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} {\text{h}}({\text{x}}) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\text{x}} \to {\text{k}}} [ - ({\text{x}} + 1)] = - ({\text{k}} + 1){/tex} Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to {\mathbf{k}}} {\text{h}}({\text{x}}) = {\text{h}}({\text{k}}){/tex} Therefore, h is continuous at all points x, i.e., x < -1 Case II : If k > -1, Then h(k) = k + 1 and {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} h(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} (x + 1) = k + 1{/tex} Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} h(x) = h(k){/tex} Therefore, h is continuous at all points x, i.e., x > -1. Case III: If k = -1, Then, h(k) = h(-1) = -1 + 1 = 0 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} h(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} {/tex} [-(x + 1)] = -(-1 + 1) = 0 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} h(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} (x + 1){/tex} = -(-1 + 1) = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} h(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} h(x) = h( - 1){/tex} Therefore, g is continuous at x = -1 From the above 3 cases, we get that h is continuous at all points. Hence, g and h are continuous function. Therefore, f = g – h is also a continuous function.
solution_html:
It is given that f(x) = |x| - |x + 1|
The given function f is defined for real number and f can be written as the composition of two functions, as
f = goh, where g(x) = |x| and h(x) = |x + 1|
Then, f = g - h
First we have to prove that g(x) = |x| and h(x) = |x + 1| are continuous functions.
g(x) = |x| can be written as
{tex}g(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} {-x, \text { if } x<0} \\ {x, \text { if } x \geq 0} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
Now, g is defined for all real number.
Let k be a real number.
Case I: If k < 0,
Then g(k) = -k
And {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} g(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} ( - x) = - k{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} g(x) = g(k){/tex}
Therefore, g is continuous at all points x, i.e., x > 0|
Case II: If k > 0,
Then g(k) = k and
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} g(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} x = k{/tex}
Thus {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to {\mathbf{k}}} {\text{g}}({\text{x}}) = {\text{g}}({\text{k}}){/tex}
Therefore, g is continuous at all points x, i.e., x < 0.
Case III: If k = 0,
Then, g(k) = g(0) = 0
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} g(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} ( - x) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} g(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} (x) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} g(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} g(x) = g(0){/tex}
Therefore, g is continuous at x = 0
From the above 3 cases, we get that g is continuous at all points.
g(x) = |x + 1| can be written as
{tex}g(x) = \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - (x + 1),{\text{ if }}x < - 1} \\ {x + 1,{\text{ if }}x \geqslant - 1} \end{array}} \right.{/tex}
Now, h is defined for all real number.
Let k be a real number.
Case I: If k < -1,
Then h(k) = -(k + 1)
And {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} {\text{h}}({\text{x}}) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\text{x}} \to {\text{k}}} [ - ({\text{x}} + 1)] = - ({\text{k}} + 1){/tex}
Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to {\mathbf{k}}} {\text{h}}({\text{x}}) = {\text{h}}({\text{k}}){/tex}
Therefore, h is continuous at all points x, i.e., x < -1
Case II: If k > -1,
Then h(k) = k + 1 and
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} h(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} (x + 1) = k + 1{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} h(x) = h(k){/tex}
Therefore, h is continuous at all points x, i.e., x > -1.
Case III: If k = -1,
Then, h(k) = h(-1) = -1 + 1 = 0
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} h(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} {/tex}[-(x + 1)] = -(-1 + 1) = 0
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} h(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} (x + 1){/tex}= -(-1 + 1) = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} h(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} h(x) = h( - 1){/tex}
Therefore, g is continuous at x = -1
From the above 3 cases, we get that h is continuous at all points.
Hence, g and h are continuous function.
Therefore, f = g – h is also a continuous function.

question_html: Differentiate the function {tex}x^{x \cos x}+\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex} w.r.t. x.
question_text: Differentiate the function {tex}x^{x \cos x}+\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex} w.r.t. x.
explanation: Given: {tex}\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{x} \cos \mathrm{x}}+\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}{\mathrm{x}^{2}-1}{/tex} Let {tex}y=x^{x \cos x}+\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex} Let y = u + v {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} u = x xcos x and v = {tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex} For, {tex}\mathrm{u}=\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{x} \cos x}{/tex} Taking log on both sides, we get {tex}\log u=\log {x} ^{x \cos x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log u=x \cdot \cos x \cdot \log x{/tex} Now, differentiate both sides with respect to x {tex}\frac{d}{d x}(\log u)=\frac{d}{d x}[x \cdot \cos x \cdot \log x]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{u}} \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\cos \mathrm{x} \cdot \log \mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\mathrm{x})+\mathrm{x} \cdot \log \mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\cos \mathrm{x})+\mathrm{x} \cdot \cos \mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \log \mathrm{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{u}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{u}\left[\cos \mathrm{x} . \log \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{x} \cdot \log \mathrm{x}(-\sin \mathrm{x})+\mathrm{x} \cdot \cos \mathrm{x} \cdot\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=x^{x \cos x}[\cos x \cdot \log x-x \cdot \log x \cdot \sin x+\cos x]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=x^{x \cos x}[\cos x(1+\log x)-x \cdot \log x \cdot \sin x]{/tex} For, v = {tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex} Takinglog on both sides, we get {tex}\log \mathrm{v}=\log \left(\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}{\mathrm{x}^{2}-1}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log v=\log \left(x^{2}+1\right)-\log \left(x^{2}-1\right){/tex} Now, differentiate both sides with respect to x {tex}\frac{d}{d x}(\log v)=\frac{d}{d x}\left[\log \left(x^{2}+1\right)-\log \left(x^{2}-1\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)-\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}^{2}-1} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=v \cdot\left[\frac{1}{x^{2}+1} \cdot(2 x)-\frac{1}{x^{2}-1} \cdot(2 x)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\left(\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}\right) \cdot\left[\frac{2 x\left(x^{2}-1\right)-2 x\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)\left(x^{2}-1\right)}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\left(\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}\right) \cdot\left[\frac{-4 x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)\left(x^{2}-1\right)}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\left[\frac{-4 x}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}\right]{/tex} Because, y = u + v {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=x^{x \cos x}[\cos x(1+\log x)-x \cdot \log x \cdot \sin x]-\left[\frac{4 x}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}\right]{/tex}
solution: Given: {tex}\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{x} \cos \mathrm{x}}+\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}{\mathrm{x}^{2}-1}{/tex} Let {tex}y=x^{x \cos x}+\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex} Let y = u + v {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} u = x xcos x and v = {tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex} For, {tex}\mathrm{u}=\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{x} \cos x}{/tex} Taking log on both sides, we get {tex}\log u=\log {x} ^{x \cos x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log u=x \cdot \cos x \cdot \log x{/tex} Now, differentiate both sides with respect to x {tex}\frac{d}{d x}(\log u)=\frac{d}{d x}[x \cdot \cos x \cdot \log x]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{u}} \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\cos \mathrm{x} \cdot \log \mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\mathrm{x})+\mathrm{x} \cdot \log \mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\cos \mathrm{x})+\mathrm{x} \cdot \cos \mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \log \mathrm{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{u}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{u}\left[\cos \mathrm{x} . \log \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{x} \cdot \log \mathrm{x}(-\sin \mathrm{x})+\mathrm{x} \cdot \cos \mathrm{x} \cdot\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=x^{x \cos x}[\cos x \cdot \log x-x \cdot \log x \cdot \sin x+\cos x]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=x^{x \cos x}[\cos x(1+\log x)-x \cdot \log x \cdot \sin x]{/tex} For, v = {tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex} Takinglog on both sides, we get {tex}\log \mathrm{v}=\log \left(\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}{\mathrm{x}^{2}-1}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log v=\log \left(x^{2}+1\right)-\log \left(x^{2}-1\right){/tex} Now, differentiate both sides with respect to x {tex}\frac{d}{d x}(\log v)=\frac{d}{d x}\left[\log \left(x^{2}+1\right)-\log \left(x^{2}-1\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)-\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}^{2}-1} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=v \cdot\left[\frac{1}{x^{2}+1} \cdot(2 x)-\frac{1}{x^{2}-1} \cdot(2 x)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\left(\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}\right) \cdot\left[\frac{2 x\left(x^{2}-1\right)-2 x\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)\left(x^{2}-1\right)}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\left(\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}\right) \cdot\left[\frac{-4 x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)\left(x^{2}-1\right)}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\left[\frac{-4 x}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}\right]{/tex} Because, y = u + v {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=x^{x \cos x}[\cos x(1+\log x)-x \cdot \log x \cdot \sin x]-\left[\frac{4 x}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: {tex}\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{x} \cos \mathrm{x}}+\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}{\mathrm{x}^{2}-1}{/tex} 
Let {tex}y=x^{x \cos x}+\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex} 
Let y = u + v
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} u = xxcos x and v = {tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex}
For, {tex}\mathrm{u}=\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{x} \cos x}{/tex} 
Taking log on both sides, we get
{tex}\log u=\log {x} ^{x \cos x}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \log u=x \cdot \cos x \cdot \log x{/tex} 
Now, differentiate both sides with respect to x
{tex}\frac{d}{d x}(\log u)=\frac{d}{d x}[x \cdot \cos x \cdot \log x]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{u}} \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\cos \mathrm{x} \cdot \log \mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\mathrm{x})+\mathrm{x} \cdot \log \mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\cos \mathrm{x})+\mathrm{x} \cdot \cos \mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \log \mathrm{x}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{u}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{u}\left[\cos \mathrm{x} . \log \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{x} \cdot \log \mathrm{x}(-\sin \mathrm{x})+\mathrm{x} \cdot \cos \mathrm{x} \cdot\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=x^{x \cos x}[\cos x \cdot \log x-x \cdot \log x \cdot \sin x+\cos x]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=x^{x \cos x}[\cos x(1+\log x)-x \cdot \log x \cdot \sin x]{/tex} 
For, v = {tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex} 
Takinglog on both sides, we get
{tex}\log \mathrm{v}=\log \left(\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}{\mathrm{x}^{2}-1}\right){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \log v=\log \left(x^{2}+1\right)-\log \left(x^{2}-1\right){/tex} 
Now, differentiate both sides with respect to x
{tex}\frac{d}{d x}(\log v)=\frac{d}{d x}\left[\log \left(x^{2}+1\right)-\log \left(x^{2}-1\right)\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)-\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}^{2}-1} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}\right){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=v \cdot\left[\frac{1}{x^{2}+1} \cdot(2 x)-\frac{1}{x^{2}-1} \cdot(2 x)\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\left(\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}\right) \cdot\left[\frac{2 x\left(x^{2}-1\right)-2 x\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)\left(x^{2}-1\right)}\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\left(\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}\right) \cdot\left[\frac{-4 x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)\left(x^{2}-1\right)}\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\left[\frac{-4 x}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}\right]{/tex} 
Because, y = u + v
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=x^{x \cos x}[\cos x(1+\log x)-x \cdot \log x \cdot \sin x]-\left[\frac{4 x}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}\right]{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}x = \sqrt {{a^{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}t}}} ,y = \sqrt {{a^{{{\cos }^{ - 1}}t}}} {/tex} show that {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{ - y}}{x}{/tex}
question_text: If {tex}x = \sqrt {{a^{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}t}}} ,y = \sqrt {{a^{{{\cos }^{ - 1}}t}}} {/tex} show that {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{ - y}}{x}{/tex}
explanation: {tex}x = \sqrt {{a^{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}t}}} {/tex} Squaring both sides, {tex}{x^2} = {a^{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}t}}{/tex} Differentiating, {tex}2x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = {a^{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}t}}.\log a\frac{1}{{\sqrt {1 - {t^2}} }}{/tex} .... (1) {tex}y = \sqrt {{a^{{{\cos }^{ - 1}}t}}} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {y^2} = {a^{{{\cos }^{ - 1}}t}}{/tex} {tex}2y\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = {a^{{{\cos }^{ - 1}}t}}\log a\frac{{ - 1}}{{\sqrt {1 - {t^2}} }}{/tex} ...(2) Dividing (2) and (1) {tex}\frac{{2y\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}}}{{2x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}}} = \frac{{{a^{{{\cos }^{ - 1}}t}}\log a\frac{{ - 1}}{{\sqrt {1 - {t^2}} }}}}{{{a^{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}t}} loga\frac{1}{{\sqrt {1 - {t^2}} }}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{y}{x}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \frac{{{a^{{{\cos }^{ - 1}}t}}}}{{{a^{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}}}t}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{y}{x}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \frac{{{y^2}}}{{{x^2}}}\left[ \begin{gathered} \because {a^{{{\cos }^{ - 1}}t}} = {y^2} \hfill \;and\; {a^{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}t}} = {x^2} \hfill \end{gathered} \right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{ - y}}{x}{/tex}
solution: {tex}x = \sqrt {{a^{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}t}}} {/tex} Squaring both sides, {tex}{x^2} = {a^{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}t}}{/tex} Differentiating, {tex}2x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = {a^{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}t}}.\log a\frac{1}{{\sqrt {1 - {t^2}} }}{/tex} .... (1) {tex}y = \sqrt {{a^{{{\cos }^{ - 1}}t}}} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {y^2} = {a^{{{\cos }^{ - 1}}t}}{/tex} {tex}2y\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = {a^{{{\cos }^{ - 1}}t}}\log a\frac{{ - 1}}{{\sqrt {1 - {t^2}} }}{/tex} ...(2) Dividing (2) and (1) {tex}\frac{{2y\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}}}{{2x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}}} = \frac{{{a^{{{\cos }^{ - 1}}t}}\log a\frac{{ - 1}}{{\sqrt {1 - {t^2}} }}}}{{{a^{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}t}} loga\frac{1}{{\sqrt {1 - {t^2}} }}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{y}{x}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \frac{{{a^{{{\cos }^{ - 1}}t}}}}{{{a^{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}}}t}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{y}{x}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \frac{{{y^2}}}{{{x^2}}}\left[ \begin{gathered} \because {a^{{{\cos }^{ - 1}}t}} = {y^2} \hfill \;and\; {a^{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}t}} = {x^2} \hfill \end{gathered} \right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{ - y}}{x}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}x = \sqrt {{a^{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}t}}} {/tex}

Squaring both sides,

{tex}{x^2} = {a^{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}t}}{/tex}

Differentiating,

{tex}2x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = {a^{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}t}}.\log a\frac{1}{{\sqrt {1 - {t^2}} }}{/tex}.... (1)

{tex}y = \sqrt {{a^{{{\cos }^{ - 1}}t}}} {/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow {y^2} = {a^{{{\cos }^{ - 1}}t}}{/tex}

{tex}2y\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = {a^{{{\cos }^{ - 1}}t}}\log a\frac{{ - 1}}{{\sqrt {1 - {t^2}} }}{/tex} ...(2)

Dividing (2) and (1)

{tex}\frac{{2y\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}}}{{2x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}}} = \frac{{{a^{{{\cos }^{ - 1}}t}}\log a\frac{{ - 1}}{{\sqrt {1 - {t^2}} }}}}{{{a^{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}t}} loga\frac{1}{{\sqrt {1 - {t^2}} }}}}{/tex}

{tex}\frac{y}{x}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \frac{{{a^{{{\cos }^{ - 1}}t}}}}{{{a^{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}}}t}}{/tex}

{tex}\frac{y}{x}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \frac{{{y^2}}}{{{x^2}}}\left[ \begin{gathered} \because {a^{{{\cos }^{ - 1}}t}} = {y^2} \hfill \;and\; {a^{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}t}} = {x^2} \hfill \end{gathered} \right]{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{ - y}}{x}{/tex}

question_html: Differentiate the function with respect to x: {tex}\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{x}+x^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}{/tex}
question_text: Differentiate the function with respect to x: {tex}\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{x}+x^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}{/tex}
explanation: Let {tex}\mathrm{y}=\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{x}+x^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}{/tex} Also, Let {tex}u=\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{x} \text { and } \mathrm{v}=x^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} y = u + v {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} ...(i) Then, {tex}\mathrm{u}=\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log u=\log \left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log u=x \log \left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right){/tex} Differentiating x}=x^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}\left(\frac{x+1-\log x}{x^{2}}\right){/tex} ....(iii) From (i), (ii) and (iii), we obtain {tex}\frac{d y}{d v=\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right) \log x{/tex} Differentiating both sides with respect to x, {tex}\frac{1}{v} \frac{d v}{d x}=\log x \frac{d}{d x}\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)+\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right) solution_html:
Let {tex}\mathrm{y}=\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{x}+x^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}{/tex}
Also, Let {tex}u=\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{x} \text { and } \mathrm{v}=x^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} y = u + v
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} ...(i)
Then, {tex}\mathrm{u}=\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \log u=\log \left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \log u=x \log \left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right){/tex}
Differentiating both sides with respect to x,
{tex}\frac{1}{u} \frac{d u}{d x}=\log \left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right) \frac{d}{d x}(x)+x \frac{d}{d x}\left[\log \left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{u} \frac{d u}{d x}=\log \left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)+x \frac{1}{\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)} \frac{d}{d x}\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=u\left[\log \left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)+\frac{x}{\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)} \times\left(1-\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{x}\left[\log \left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)+\frac{\left(x-\frac{1}{x}\right)}{\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{x}\left[\log \left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)+\frac{x^{2}-1}{x^{2}+1}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{x}\left[\frac{x^{2}-1}{x^{2}+1}+\log \left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)\right]{/tex}
Again, {tex}v=x^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \log v=\log \left[x^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \log v=\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right) \log x{/tex}
Differentiating both sides with respect to x,
{tex}\frac{1}{v} \frac{d v}{d x}=\log x \frac{d}{d x}\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)+\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right) \frac{d}{d x} \log x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{v} \frac{d v}{d x}=\left(-\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right) \log x+\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)\left(\frac{1}{x}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{v} \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{\log x}{x^{2}}+\frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{x^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=v\left[\frac{-\log x+x+1}{x^{2}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=x^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}\left(\frac{x+1-\log x}{x^{2}}\right){/tex} ....(iii)
From (i), (ii) and (iii), we obtain
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{x}\left[\frac{x^{2}-1}{x^{2}+1}+\log \left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)\right]+x^{\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)}\left(\frac{x+1-\log x}{x^{2}}\right){/tex}

question_html: Find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} when y = xsin x + (sin x)x
question_text: Find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} when y = x sin x + (sin x) x
explanation: Let y = x sin x + (sin x) x Also, let u = x sin x and v = (sin x) x {tex}\therefore{/tex} y = u + v {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} ...(i) Now, u = x sin x Taking log on both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = log (x sin x ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = sin x log x Differentiating both sides with respect to x, {tex}\frac{1}{u} \frac{d u}{d x}=\log x \frac{d}{d x}(\sin x)+\sin x \frac{d}{d x}(\log x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=u\left[\cos x \log x+\sin x \frac{1}{x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=x^{\sin x}\left[\cos x \log x+\frac{\sin x}{x}\right]{/tex} ...(ii) Again, v = (sin x) x taking log on both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log v = log (sin x) x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log v = x log(sin x) Differentiating both sides with respect to x, {tex}\frac{1}{v} \frac{d v}{d x}=\log (\sin x) \frac{d}{d x}(x)+x \frac{d}{d x}[\log (\sin x)]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=v\left[\log (\sin x)+x \frac{1}{\sin x} \frac{d}{d x}(\sin x)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=(\sin x)^{x}\left[\log \sin x+\frac{x}{\sin x} \cos x\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = (sin x) x [log sin x + x cot x] ...(iii) From (i), (ii) and (iii), we obtain {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=x^{\sin x}\left(\cos x \log x+\frac{\sin x}{x}\right){/tex} + (sin x) x [log sin x + x cot x].
solution: Let y = x sin x + (sin x) x Also, let u = x sin x and v = (sin x) x {tex}\therefore{/tex} y = u + v {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} ...(i) Now, u = x sin x Taking log on both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = log (x sin x ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = sin x log x Differentiating both sides with respect to x, {tex}\frac{1}{u} \frac{d u}{d x}=\log x \frac{d}{d x}(\sin x)+\sin x \frac{d}{d x}(\log x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=u\left[\cos x \log x+\sin x \frac{1}{x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=x^{\sin x}\left[\cos x \log x+\frac{\sin x}{x}\right]{/tex} ...(ii) Again, v = (sin x) x taking log on both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log v = log (sin x) x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log v = x log(sin x) Differentiating both sides with respect to x, {tex}\frac{1}{v} \frac{d v}{d x}=\log (\sin x) \frac{d}{d x}(x)+x \frac{d}{d x}[\log (\sin x)]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=v\left[\log (\sin x)+x \frac{1}{\sin x} \frac{d}{d x}(\sin x)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=(\sin x)^{x}\left[\log \sin x+\frac{x}{\sin x} \cos x\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = (sin x) x [log sin x + x cot x] ...(iii) From (i), (ii) and (iii), we obtain {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=x^{\sin x}\left(\cos x \log x+\frac{\sin x}{x}\right){/tex} + (sin x) x [log sin x + x cot x].
solution_html:
Let y = xsin x + (sin x)x
Also, let u = xsin x and v = (sin x)x
{tex}\therefore{/tex} y = u + v
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} ...(i)
Now, u = xsin x
Taking log on both sides,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = log (xsin x)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = sin x log x
Differentiating both sides with respect to x,
{tex}\frac{1}{u} \frac{d u}{d x}=\log x \frac{d}{d x}(\sin x)+\sin x \frac{d}{d x}(\log x){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=u\left[\cos x \log x+\sin x \frac{1}{x}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=x^{\sin x}\left[\cos x \log x+\frac{\sin x}{x}\right]{/tex} ...(ii)
Again, v = (sin x)x
taking log on both sides,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log v =  log (sin x)x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log v = x log(sin x)
Differentiating both sides with respect to x,
{tex}\frac{1}{v} \frac{d v}{d x}=\log (\sin x) \frac{d}{d x}(x)+x \frac{d}{d x}[\log (\sin x)]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=v\left[\log (\sin x)+x \frac{1}{\sin x} \frac{d}{d x}(\sin x)\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=(\sin x)^{x}\left[\log \sin x+\frac{x}{\sin x} \cos x\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = (sin x)x [log sin x + x cot x] ...(iii)
From (i), (ii) and (iii), we obtain
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=x^{\sin x}\left(\cos x \log x+\frac{\sin x}{x}\right){/tex} + (sin x)x [log sin x + x cot x].

question_html:
Find all points of discontinuity if

{tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \left| x \right| + 3,\,\,if\,\,x \leqslant - 3 \hfill \\ - 2x,\,\,if - 3 < x < 3 \hfill \\ 6x + 2,\,\,if\,\,x \geqslant 3 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex}
question_text: Find all points of discontinuity if {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \left| x \right| + 3,\,\,if\,\,x \leqslant - 3 \hfill \\ - 2x,\,\,if - 3 < x < 3 \hfill \\ 6x + 2,\,\,if\,\,x \geqslant 3 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex}
explanation: At x = - 3 f (-3) = |-3| + 3 = 3 + 3 = 6 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {-3^ - }} f(x) ={/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {-3^ -}}( |x|+3){/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} {/tex} (|-3 - h| + 3) = |-3 - 0| + 3 = |3| + 3 = 3 + 3 = 6 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {-3^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {-3^ + }} (-2x){/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} - 2\left( { - 3 + h} \right){/tex} = 6 {tex}\therefore \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - {3^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - {3^ + }} f(x) = f(-3){/tex} Hence, f(x) is Continuous at x = -3. At x = 3 {tex}f(3) = 6 \times 3 + 2 = 20{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ - }} \left( { - 2x} \right){/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} - 2\left( {3 - h} \right){/tex} = -6 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ + }} \left( {6x + 2} \right){/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \left[ {6\left( {3 + h} \right) + 2} \right]{/tex} = 20 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ - }} f(x) \ne \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ + }} f(x){/tex} Hence f(x) is discontinuous at x = 3.
solution: At x = - 3 f (-3) = |-3| + 3 = 3 + 3 = 6 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {-3^ - }} f(x) ={/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {-3^ -}}( |x|+3){/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} {/tex} (|-3 - h| + 3) = |-3 - 0| + 3 = |3| + 3 = 3 + 3 = 6 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {-3^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {-3^ + }} (-2x){/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} - 2\left( { - 3 + h} \right){/tex} = 6 {tex}\therefore \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - {3^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - {3^ + }} f(x) = f(-3){/tex} Hence, f(x) is Continuous at x = -3. At x = 3 {tex}f(3) = 6 \times 3 + 2 = 20{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ - }} \left( { - 2x} \right){/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} - 2\left( {3 - h} \right){/tex} = -6 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ + }} \left( {6x + 2} \right){/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \left[ {6\left( {3 + h} \right) + 2} \right]{/tex} = 20 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ - }} f(x) \ne \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ + }} f(x){/tex} Hence f(x) is discontinuous at x = 3.
solution_html:
At x = - 3

f (-3) = |-3| + 3 = 3 + 3 = 6

{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {-3^ - }} f(x) ={/tex}{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {-3^ -}}( |x|+3){/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} {/tex}(|-3 - h| + 3) = |-3 - 0| + 3 = |3| + 3 = 3 + 3 = 6

{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {-3^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {-3^ + }} (-2x){/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} - 2\left( { - 3 + h} \right){/tex} = 6

{tex}\therefore \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - {3^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - {3^ + }} f(x) = f(-3){/tex}

Hence, f(x) is Continuous at x = -3.

At x = 3

{tex}f(3) = 6 \times 3 + 2 = 20{/tex}

{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ - }} \left( { - 2x} \right){/tex}

= {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} - 2\left( {3 - h} \right){/tex}

= -6

{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ + }} \left( {6x + 2} \right){/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \left[ {6\left( {3 + h} \right) + 2} \right]{/tex}

= 20

{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ - }} f(x) \ne \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ + }} f(x){/tex}

Hence f(x) is discontinuous at x = 3.

solution: We have, {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}},{/tex} if {tex}y = {x^{\tan x}} + \sqrt {\frac{{{x^2} + 1}}{2}} {/tex} Taking, {tex}u = {x^{\tan x}}{/tex} and {tex}v = \sqrt {\frac{{{x^2} + 1}}{2}} {/tex} {tex}\log u = \tan x\log x{/tex} ...(ii) and {tex}{v^2} = \frac{{{x^2} + 1}}{2}{/tex} ...(iii) On, differentiating Eq. (ii) w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{1}{u}.\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = \tan x.\frac{1}{x} + \log x{\sec ^2}x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{du}}{{dx}} = u\left[ {\frac{{\tan x}}{x} + \log x.{{\sec }^2}x} \right]{/tex} {tex} = {x^{\tan x}}\left[ {\frac{{\tan x}}{x} + \log x.{{\sec }^2}x} \right]{/tex} ...(iv) also, differentiating Eq. (iii) w.r.t. x, we get {tex}2v.\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{2}\left( {2x} \right) \Rightarrow \frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{{4v}}.\left( {2x} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{{4.\sqrt {\frac{{{x^2} + 1}}{2}} }}.2x = \frac{{x.\sqrt 2 }}{{2\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{x}{{\sqrt {2\left( {{x^2} + 1} \right)} }}{/tex} ...(v) Now, {tex}y = u + v{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{du}}{{dx}} + \frac{{dv}}{{dx}}{/tex} {tex}= {x^{\tan x}}\left[ {\frac{{\tan x}}{x} + \log x.{{\sec }^2}x} \right] + \frac{x}{{\sqrt {2\left( {{x^2} + 1} \right)} }}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have, {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}},{/tex}if {tex}y = {x^{\tan x}} + \sqrt {\frac{{{x^2} + 1}}{2}} {/tex}

Taking, {tex}u = {x^{\tan x}}{/tex} and {tex}v = \sqrt {\frac{{{x^2} + 1}}{2}} {/tex}

{tex}\log u = \tan x\log x{/tex} ...(ii)

and {tex}{v^2} = \frac{{{x^2} + 1}}{2}{/tex}...(iii)

On, differentiating Eq. (ii) w.r.t. x, we get

{tex}\frac{1}{u}.\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = \tan x.\frac{1}{x} + \log x{\sec ^2}x{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{du}}{{dx}} = u\left[ {\frac{{\tan x}}{x} + \log x.{{\sec }^2}x} \right]{/tex}

{tex} = {x^{\tan x}}\left[ {\frac{{\tan x}}{x} + \log x.{{\sec }^2}x} \right]{/tex} ...(iv)

also, differentiating Eq. (iii) w.r.t. x, we get

{tex}2v.\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{2}\left( {2x} \right) \Rightarrow \frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{{4v}}.\left( {2x} \right){/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{{4.\sqrt {\frac{{{x^2} + 1}}{2}} }}.2x = \frac{{x.\sqrt 2 }}{{2\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{x}{{\sqrt {2\left( {{x^2} + 1} \right)} }}{/tex} ...(v)

Now, {tex}y = u + v{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{du}}{{dx}} + \frac{{dv}}{{dx}}{/tex}

{tex}= {x^{\tan x}}\left[ {\frac{{\tan x}}{x} + \log x.{{\sec }^2}x} \right] + \frac{x}{{\sqrt {2\left( {{x^2} + 1} \right)} }}{/tex}

question_html: If {tex} y\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - \log \left( {\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x} \right)=0{/tex} prove that {tex} \left( {{x^2} + 1} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + xy + 1 = 0{/tex}
question_text: If {tex} y\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - \log \left( {\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x} \right)=0{/tex} prove that {tex} \left( {{x^2} + 1} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + xy + 1 = 0{/tex}
explanation: {tex} y\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - \log \left( {\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x} \right)=0{/tex} differentiating both sides w.r.t x {tex} y.\frac{1}{{2\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}(2x) + \sqrt {{x^2} + 1} .\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} -{/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x}}\left[ {\frac{{1(2x)}}{{2\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }} - 1} \right]=0{/tex} {tex} \frac{{xy}}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }} + \sqrt {{x^2} + 1} .\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - \frac{1}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x}}\left[ {\frac{{x - \sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}} \right] =0{/tex} {tex} \frac{{xy + ({x^2} + 1)}}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{ - \left( {\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x} \right)}}{{ \left( {\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x} \right)\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}{/tex} {tex} xy + ({x^2} + 1)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - 1{/tex} {tex} ({x^2} + 1)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + xy + 1 = 0{/tex}
solution: {tex} y\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - \log \left( {\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x} \right)=0{/tex} differentiating both sides w.r.t x {tex} y.\frac{1}{{2\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}(2x) + \sqrt {{x^2} + 1} .\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} -{/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x}}\left[ {\frac{{1(2x)}}{{2\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }} - 1} \right]=0{/tex} {tex} \frac{{xy}}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }} + \sqrt {{x^2} + 1} .\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - \frac{1}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x}}\left[ {\frac{{x - \sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}} \right] =0{/tex} {tex} \frac{{xy + ({x^2} + 1)}}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{ - \left( {\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x} \right)}}{{ \left( {\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x} \right)\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}{/tex} {tex} xy + ({x^2} + 1)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - 1{/tex} {tex} ({x^2} + 1)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + xy + 1 = 0{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} y\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - \log \left( {\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x} \right)=0{/tex}

differentiating both sides w.r.t x

{tex} y.\frac{1}{{2\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}(2x) + \sqrt {{x^2} + 1} .\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} -{/tex}{tex} \frac{1}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x}}\left[ {\frac{{1(2x)}}{{2\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }} - 1} \right]=0{/tex}

{tex} \frac{{xy}}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }} + \sqrt {{x^2} + 1} .\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - \frac{1}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x}}\left[ {\frac{{x - \sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}} \right] =0{/tex}

{tex} \frac{{xy + ({x^2} + 1)}}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{ - \left( {\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x} \right)}}{{ \left( {\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x} \right)\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}{/tex}

{tex} xy + ({x^2} + 1)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - 1{/tex}

{tex} ({x^2} + 1)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + xy + 1 = 0{/tex}

question_html: Differentiate {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}\right){/tex} with respect to {tex}\sin ^{-1}(2 x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}){/tex}, if {tex}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}<x<\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}.
question_text: Differentiate {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}\right){/tex} with respect to {tex}\sin ^{-1}(2 x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}){/tex} , if {tex}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}<x<\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} .
explanation: Let {tex}\mathbf{u}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}\right){/tex} Put x = sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = sin -1 x {tex}\Rightarrow u=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin \theta}{\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta}}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow u=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} u = tan -1 (tan {tex}\theta{/tex} ) ...(i) And Let {tex}{v}=\sin ^{-1}(2 x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}){/tex} {tex}v=\sin ^{-1}(2 \sin \theta \sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta}){/tex} v = sin -1 (2 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ) v = sin -1 (sin 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} ) ...(ii) Here, {tex}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}<x<\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}<\sin \theta<\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{\pi}{4}<\theta<\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} So, from equation (i) {tex}u=\theta\left[\text { since, } \tan ^{-1}(\tan \theta)=\theta, \text { if } \theta \in\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} u = sin -1 x Differentiating it with respect to x, {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} ....(iii) from equation (ii), {tex}v=2 \theta\left[\text { since, } \sin ^{-1}(\sin \theta)=\theta, \text { if } \theta \in\left[-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v = 2sin -1 x Differentiating it with respect to x, {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} ...(iv) Dividing equation (iii) by (iv), {tex}\frac{\frac{d u}{d x}}{\frac{d v}{d x}}=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}\right)\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} .
solution: Let {tex}\mathbf{u}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}\right){/tex} Put x = sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = sin -1 x {tex}\Rightarrow u=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin \theta}{\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta}}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow u=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} u = tan -1 (tan {tex}\theta{/tex} ) ...(i) And Let {tex}{v}=\sin ^{-1}(2 x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}){/tex} {tex}v=\sin ^{-1}(2 \sin \theta \sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta}){/tex} v = sin -1 (2 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ) v = sin -1 (sin 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} ) ...(ii) Here, {tex}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}<x<\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}<\sin \theta<\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{\pi}{4}<\theta<\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} So, from equation (i) {tex}u=\theta\left[\text { since, } \tan ^{-1}(\tan \theta)=\theta, \text { if } \theta \in\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} u = sin -1 x Differentiating it with respect to x, {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} ....(iii) from equation (ii), {tex}v=2 \theta\left[\text { since, } \sin ^{-1}(\sin \theta)=\theta, \text { if } \theta \in\left[-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v = 2sin -1 x Differentiating it with respect to x, {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} ...(iv) Dividing equation (iii) by (iv), {tex}\frac{\frac{d u}{d x}}{\frac{d v}{d x}}=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}\right)\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} .
solution_html:
Let {tex}\mathbf{u}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}\right){/tex}
Put x = sin{tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = sin-1x
{tex}\Rightarrow u=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin \theta}{\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow u=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} u = tan-1(tan {tex}\theta{/tex}) ...(i)
And
Let {tex}{v}=\sin ^{-1}(2 x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}){/tex}
{tex}v=\sin ^{-1}(2 \sin \theta \sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta}){/tex}
v = sin-1(2 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex})
v = sin-1(sin 2{tex}\theta{/tex}) ...(ii)
Here,
{tex}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}<x<\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}<\sin \theta<\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{\pi}{4}<\theta<\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
So, from equation (i)
{tex}u=\theta\left[\text { since, } \tan ^{-1}(\tan \theta)=\theta, \text { if } \theta \in\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} u = sin-1x
Differentiating it with respect to x,
{tex}\frac{d u}{d x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} ....(iii)
from equation (ii),
{tex}v=2 \theta\left[\text { since, } \sin ^{-1}(\sin \theta)=\theta, \text { if } \theta \in\left[-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v = 2sin-1x
Differentiating it with respect to x,
{tex}\frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} ...(iv)
Dividing equation (iii) by (iv),
{tex}\frac{\frac{d u}{d x}}{\frac{d v}{d x}}=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}\right)\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}.

question_html: {tex}x = a\left( {\cos t + \log \tan \frac{t}{2}} \right),{/tex}{tex}y = a\sin t{/tex} find {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex}
question_text: {tex}x = a\left( {\cos t + \log \tan \frac{t}{2}} \right),{/tex} {tex}y = a\sin t{/tex} find {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex}
explanation: {tex}x = a\left( {\cos t + \log \tan \frac{t}{2}} \right){/tex} {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = a\left[ { - \sin t+\frac{1}{{\tan \frac{t}{2}}}.{{\sec }^2}\frac{t}{2}.\frac{1}{2}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = a\left[ { - \sin t + \frac{1}{{\frac{{\sin \frac{t}{2}}}{{\cos \frac{t}{2}}}}} \times \frac{1}{{{{\cos }^2}\frac{t}{2}}} \times \frac{1}{2}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = a\left[ { - \sin t + \frac{1}{{2\sin \frac{t}{2}.\cos \frac{t}{2}}}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = a\left[ { - \sin t + \frac{1}{{\sin t}}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = a\left[ {\frac{{ - {{\sin }^2}t + 1}}{{\sin t}}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = a\left[ {\frac{{{{\cos }^2}t}}{{\sin t}}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = a\frac{{{{\cos }^2}t}}{{\sin t}}{/tex} y = a sin t {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = a\cos t{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{a\cos t}}{{a\frac{{{{\cos }^2}t}}{{\sin t}}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{\sin t}}{{\cos t}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \tan t{/tex}
solution: {tex}x = a\left( {\cos t + \log \tan \frac{t}{2}} \right){/tex} {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = a\left[ { - \sin t+\frac{1}{{\tan \frac{t}{2}}}.{{\sec }^2}\frac{t}{2}.\frac{1}{2}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = a\left[ { - \sin t + \frac{1}{{\frac{{\sin \frac{t}{2}}}{{\cos \frac{t}{2}}}}} \times \frac{1}{{{{\cos }^2}\frac{t}{2}}} \times \frac{1}{2}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = a\left[ { - \sin t + \frac{1}{{2\sin \frac{t}{2}.\cos \frac{t}{2}}}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = a\left[ { - \sin t + \frac{1}{{\sin t}}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = a\left[ {\frac{{ - {{\sin }^2}t + 1}}{{\sin t}}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = a\left[ {\frac{{{{\cos }^2}t}}{{\sin t}}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = a\frac{{{{\cos }^2}t}}{{\sin t}}{/tex} y = a sin t {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = a\cos t{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{a\cos t}}{{a\frac{{{{\cos }^2}t}}{{\sin t}}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{\sin t}}{{\cos t}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \tan t{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}x = a\left( {\cos t + \log \tan \frac{t}{2}} \right){/tex}

{tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = a\left[ { - \sin t+\frac{1}{{\tan \frac{t}{2}}}.{{\sec }^2}\frac{t}{2}.\frac{1}{2}} \right]{/tex}

{tex} = a\left[ { - \sin t + \frac{1}{{\frac{{\sin \frac{t}{2}}}{{\cos \frac{t}{2}}}}} \times \frac{1}{{{{\cos }^2}\frac{t}{2}}} \times \frac{1}{2}} \right]{/tex}

{tex} = a\left[ { - \sin t + \frac{1}{{2\sin \frac{t}{2}.\cos \frac{t}{2}}}} \right]{/tex}

{tex} = a\left[ { - \sin t + \frac{1}{{\sin t}}} \right]{/tex}

{tex} = a\left[ {\frac{{ - {{\sin }^2}t + 1}}{{\sin t}}} \right]{/tex}

{tex} = a\left[ {\frac{{{{\cos }^2}t}}{{\sin t}}} \right]{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = a\frac{{{{\cos }^2}t}}{{\sin t}}{/tex}

y = a sin t

{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = a\cos t{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{a\cos t}}{{a\frac{{{{\cos }^2}t}}{{\sin t}}}}{/tex}{tex} = \frac{{\sin t}}{{\cos t}}{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \tan t{/tex}

question_html: Discuss the continuity and differentiability {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} {1-x} & {, \quad x<1} \\ {(1-x)(2-x),} & {1 \leq x \leq 2} \\ {3-x} & {, \quad x>2} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
question_text: Discuss the continuity and differentiability {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} {1-x} & {, \quad x<1} \\ {(1-x)(2-x),} & {1 \leq x \leq 2} \\ {3-x} & {, \quad x>2} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
explanation: When x < 1, we have f(x) = 1 - x. We know that a polynomial function is everywhere continuous and differentiable. So, f (x) is continuous and differentiable for all x < 1 Similarly, f (x) is continuous and differentiable for all x {tex}\in{/tex} (1, 2) and x > 2. Thus, the possible points where we have to check the continuity and differentiability of f (x) are x = 1 and x = 2. Continuity at x = 1: We observe that: {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1}(1-x){/tex} {tex}[\because f(x)=1-x \text { for } x<1]{/tex} = 1 - 1 = 0 {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1}(1-x)(2-x){/tex} =0 {tex}[\because f(x)=(1-x)(2-x), \text { for } 1 \leq x \leq 2]{/tex} and, f(1) = (1 - 1)(2 - 1) = 0 {tex}\therefore \quad \lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} f(x)=f(1){/tex} So, f(x) is continuous at x = 1. Continuity at x - 2: We observe that: {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2^{-}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2}(1-x)(2-x){/tex} {tex}[\because f(x)=(1-x)(2-x) \text { for } 1 \leq x \leq 2]{/tex} and, {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2^{+}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2}(3-x)=3-2=1{/tex} {tex}[f(x)=3-x \text { for } x>2]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2^{-}} f(x) \neq \lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2} f(x){/tex} So, f (x) is not continuous at x = 2. Differentiability at x = 1: we observe that: {tex}(\mathrm{LHD} \text { at } x=1)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} \frac{f(x)-f(1)}{x-1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad(\mathrm{LHD} \text { at } x=1)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{(1-x)-0}{x-1}{/tex} [Using definition of f(x)] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad(\mathrm{LHD} \text { at } x=1)=-\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{x-1}{x-1}=-1{/tex} and {tex}(\mathrm{RHD} \text { at } x=1)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} \frac{f(x)-f(1)}{x-1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad(\mathrm{RHD} \text { at } x=1)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{(1-x)(2-x)-0}{x-1}{/tex} [Using definition of f(x)[ {tex}\Rightarrow \quad(\mathrm{RHD} \text { at } x=1)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{(x-1)(x-2)}{x-1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad(\mathrm{RHD} \text { at } x=1)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1} x-2=1-2=-1{/tex} Clearly, (LHD at x = 1) = (RHD at x = 1). So, f(x) is differentiable at x = 1. Differentiability at x = 2: Since, f (x) is not continuous at x = 2. So, it is not differentiable at x = 2.
solution: When x < 1, we have f(x) = 1 - x. We know that a polynomial function is everywhere continuous and differentiable. So, f (x) is continuous and differentiable for all x < 1 Similarly, f (x) is continuous and differentiable for all x {tex}\in{/tex} (1, 2) and x > 2. Thus, the possible points where we have to check the continuity and differentiability of f (x) are x = 1 and x = 2. Continuity at x = 1: We observe that: {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1}(1-x){/tex} {tex}[\because f(x)=1-x \text { for } x<1]{/tex} = 1 - 1 = 0 {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1}(1-x)(2-x){/tex} =0 {tex}[\because f(x)=(1-x)(2-x), \text { for } 1 \leq x \leq 2]{/tex} and, f(1) = (1 - 1)(2 - 1) = 0 {tex}\therefore \quad \lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} f(x)=f(1){/tex} So, f(x) is continuous at x = 1. Continuity at x - 2: We observe that: {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2^{-}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2}(1-x)(2-x){/tex} {tex}[\because f(x)=(1-x)(2-x) \text { for } 1 \leq x \leq 2]{/tex} and, {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2^{+}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2}(3-x)=3-2=1{/tex} {tex}[f(x)=3-x \text { for } x>2]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2^{-}} f(x) \neq \lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2} f(x){/tex} So, f (x) is not continuous at x = 2. Differentiability at x = 1: we observe that: {tex}(\mathrm{LHD} \text { at } x=1)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} \frac{f(x)-f(1)}{x-1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad(\mathrm{LHD} \text { at } x=1)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{(1-x)-0}{x-1}{/tex} [Using definition of f(x)] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad(\mathrm{LHD} \text { at } x=1)=-\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{x-1}{x-1}=-1{/tex} and {tex}(\mathrm{RHD} \text { at } x=1)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} \frac{f(x)-f(1)}{x-1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad(\mathrm{RHD} \text { at } x=1)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{(1-x)(2-x)-0}{x-1}{/tex} [Using definition of f(x)[ {tex}\Rightarrow \quad(\mathrm{RHD} \text { at } x=1)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{(x-1)(x-2)}{x-1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad(\mathrm{RHD} \text { at } x=1)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1} x-2=1-2=-1{/tex} Clearly, (LHD at x = 1) = (RHD at x = 1). So, f(x) is differentiable at x = 1. Differentiability at x = 2: Since, f (x) is not continuous at x = 2. So, it is not differentiable at x = 2.
solution_html:
When x < 1, we have f(x) = 1 - x.
We know that a polynomial function is everywhere continuous and differentiable. So, f (x) is
continuous and differentiable for all x < 1
Similarly, f (x) is continuous and differentiable for all x {tex}\in{/tex} (1, 2) and x > 2.
Thus, the possible points where we have to check the continuity and differentiability of f (x) are
x = 1 and x = 2.
Continuity at x = 1:
We observe that:
{tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1}(1-x){/tex} {tex}[\because f(x)=1-x \text { for } x<1]{/tex}
= 1 - 1 = 0
{tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1}(1-x)(2-x){/tex}=0 {tex}[\because f(x)=(1-x)(2-x), \text { for } 1 \leq x \leq 2]{/tex}
and, f(1) = (1 - 1)(2 - 1) = 0
{tex}\therefore \quad \lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} f(x)=f(1){/tex}
So, f(x) is continuous at x = 1.
Continuity at x - 2: We observe that:
{tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2^{-}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2}(1-x)(2-x){/tex} {tex}[\because f(x)=(1-x)(2-x) \text { for } 1 \leq x \leq 2]{/tex}
and, {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2^{+}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2}(3-x)=3-2=1{/tex} {tex}[f(x)=3-x \text { for } x>2]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2^{-}} f(x) \neq \lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2} f(x){/tex}
So, f (x) is not continuous at x = 2.
Differentiability at x = 1:
we observe that:
{tex}(\mathrm{LHD} \text { at } x=1)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} \frac{f(x)-f(1)}{x-1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad(\mathrm{LHD} \text { at } x=1)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{(1-x)-0}{x-1}{/tex}  [Using definition of f(x)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad(\mathrm{LHD} \text { at } x=1)=-\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{x-1}{x-1}=-1{/tex}
and  {tex}(\mathrm{RHD} \text { at } x=1)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} \frac{f(x)-f(1)}{x-1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad(\mathrm{RHD} \text { at } x=1)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{(1-x)(2-x)-0}{x-1}{/tex} [Using definition of f(x)[
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad(\mathrm{RHD} \text { at } x=1)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{(x-1)(x-2)}{x-1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad(\mathrm{RHD} \text { at } x=1)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1} x-2=1-2=-1{/tex}
Clearly, (LHD at x = 1) = (RHD at x = 1). So, f(x) is differentiable at x = 1.
Differentiability at x = 2:
Since, f (x) is not continuous at x = 2. So, it is not differentiable at x = 2.

question_html: If y = 3cos(logx) + 4sin(logx). Show that x2y2 + xy1 + y = 0{tex}{x^2}{y_2} + x{y_1} + y = 0{/tex}
question_text: If y = 3cos(logx) + 4sin(logx). Show that x 2 y 2 + xy 1 + y = 0 {tex}{x^2}{y_2} + x{y_1} + y = 0{/tex}
explanation: y = 3cos(log x) + 4 sin(log x) Diff. both side w.r.t. to x {tex}{y_1} = \frac{{ - 3\sin \left( {\log x} \right)}}{x} + \frac{{4\cos \left( {\log x} \right)}}{x}{/tex} xy 1 = -3sin(log x) + 4cos(logx) Again diff. {tex}x{y_2} + {y_1}.1 = \frac{{ - 3\cos \left( {\log x} \right)}}{x} - \frac{{4\sin \left( {\log x} \right)}}{x}{/tex} x 2 y 2 + xy 1 = 3cos(log x) - 4sin(log x) x 2 y 2 + xy 1 = -y x 2 y 2 + xy 1 + y = 0
solution: y = 3cos(log x) + 4 sin(log x) Diff. both side w.r.t. to x {tex}{y_1} = \frac{{ - 3\sin \left( {\log x} \right)}}{x} + \frac{{4\cos \left( {\log x} \right)}}{x}{/tex} xy 1 = -3sin(log x) + 4cos(logx) Again diff. {tex}x{y_2} + {y_1}.1 = \frac{{ - 3\cos \left( {\log x} \right)}}{x} - \frac{{4\sin \left( {\log x} \right)}}{x}{/tex} x 2 y 2 + xy 1 = 3cos(log x) - 4sin(log x) x 2 y 2 + xy 1 = -y x 2 y 2 + xy 1 + y = 0
solution_html:
y = 3cos(log x) + 4 sin(log x)

Diff. both side w.r.t. to x

{tex}{y_1} = \frac{{ - 3\sin \left( {\log x} \right)}}{x} + \frac{{4\cos \left( {\log x} \right)}}{x}{/tex}

xy1 = -3sin(log x) + 4cos(logx)

Again diff.

{tex}x{y_2} + {y_1}.1 = \frac{{ - 3\cos \left( {\log x} \right)}}{x} - \frac{{4\sin \left( {\log x} \right)}}{x}{/tex}

x2y2 + xy1 = 3cos(log x) - 4sin(log x)

x2y2 + xy1 = -y

x2y2 + xy1 + y = 0
